revisions.rationale: In English syntax, the direct object ('pourers') typically precedes the prepositional phrase ('to him') unless the direct object is unusually long or complex. 'Send pourers to him' flows much more naturally while preserving the exact meaning.

revisions.from: have made a cry heard
revisions.to: make their cry heard
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'have made a cry heard' is somewhat wooden and clunky in English. Rendering the Hiphil of שמע with זעקה as 'make their cry heard' or 'sound a cry' flows much more naturally while still preserving the causative force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: against them.
revisions.to: against you.
revisions.rationale: The shift to 'them' reflects the Kethiv (written text), but the Qere (marginal reading) is עֲלֵיכֶם ('against you'). The drafter incorrectly applied the 'preserve theological tension' rule to what is actually a standard Kethiv/Qere textual variant. Following the Qere is standard practice here and resolves the jarring pronoun shift in English.

revisions.from: His offspring is destroyed, and his brothers and his neighbors—and he is no more.
revisions.to: His offspring, his brothers, and his neighbors are destroyed, and he is no more.
revisions.rationale: In Hebrew, it is common for a singular verb preceding a compound subject to agree only with the first noun. In English, however, this leaves 'his brothers and his neighbors' dangling without a clear verb. Grouping the compound subject with a plural verb (or using 'along with') resolves the awkwardness.

revisions.from: The terror you inspire has deceived you, the arrogance of your heart—
revisions.to: The terror you inspire and the arrogance of your heart have deceived you, O
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing leaves 'the arrogance of your heart' dangling as an awkward appositive or afterthought. Because the Hebrew verb (הִשִּׁיא) is masculine singular, 'the arrogance of your heart' (masculine) is likely the grammatical subject, with 'your terror' (feminine) acting as a topical phrase ('As for the terror you inspire, the arrogance of your heart has deceived you'). Alternatively, treating them as a compound subject in English ('The terror you inspire and the arrogance of your heart have deceived you') resolves the awkwardness smoothly.

revisions.from: There is anxiety like the sea; it cannot be quiet.
revisions.to: There is anxiety by the sea; it cannot be quiet.
revisions.rationale: "Anxiety like the sea" is an awkward comparison in English (comparing an emotion to a body of water). Furthermore, translating בַּיָּם as "like the sea" assumes an emendation to כַּיָּם. Rendering it "by the sea" or "in the sea" follows the MT more closely and avoids the awkward phrasing, while the footnote can explain the interpretive difficulty.

revisions.from: and their many livestock spoil
revisions.to: and their many livestock will become spoil
revisions.rationale: Because 'livestock' can take a plural verb, 'spoil' is easily misread as a verb (meaning to rot or perish) rather than a noun parallel to 'plunder.' Repeating the verb or adding an article ('a spoil') resolves the ambiguity.

revisions.from: How has the city of praise not been abandoned
revisions.to: How is it that the city of praise has not been abandoned
revisions.rationale: In English, 'How has the city... not been abandoned' can be slightly ambiguous, potentially sounding like a question about the *manner* of its non-abandonment rather than an expression of astonishment ('How does it happen that...'). Expanding to 'How is it that...' clarifies the rhetorical force of אֵיךְ in this context.

revisions.from: that you have taken
revisions.to: that you have taken over
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'cities that you have taken' strongly implies military conquest. Adding 'over' (or using 'occupied' / 'taken possession of') better expresses the idea of the remnant settling into and taking possession of the abandoned towns.

revisions.from: the helplessness of their hands
revisions.to: the feebleness of their hands
revisions.rationale: In English, 'helplessness' is typically attributed to a person rather than to body parts. 'Feebleness,' 'weakness,' or 'limpness' pairs much more naturally with 'hands' while accurately reflecting the Hebrew רִפְיוֹן (slackness/feebleness).

revisions.from: from the city was gone
revisions.to: in the city was gone
revisions.rationale: While 'from' translates the Hebrew preposition 'min' literally, 'all the bread in the city' (or 'all the bread was gone from the city') is more natural in English and avoids making 'from the city' sound like an adjective phrase modifying 'bread'.

revisions.from: Whether it is good or whether it is bad
revisions.to: Whether it is good or bad
revisions.rationale: Repeating 'whether it is' is slightly wordy in English. 'Whether it is good or bad' accurately translates the Hebrew paired conjunction (אִם ... וְאִם) while flowing more naturally.

revisions.from: None of them shall have a survivor or an escapee
revisions.to: They shall have no survivor or escapee
revisions.rationale: Translating the prepositional phrase לָהֶם distributively as 'None of them' creates an awkward logical image in English (an individual man possessing a survivor of himself). Translating it collectively as 'They shall have no...' correctly applies the concept of survival to the group as a whole.

revisions.from: concerning you
revisions.to: to you
revisions.rationale: The preposition עַל following דִּבֶּר frequently interchanges with אֶל to mean 'to'. Because the following quote ('Do not go to Egypt') is a second-person direct address, translating עֲלֵיכֶם as 'to you' is much more natural and avoids the awkward shift in perspective created by 'concerning you'.

revisions.from: which they did
revisions.to: which they committed
revisions.rationale: In English, one typically 'commits' evil deeds rather than 'does' them. Using 'committed' improves the collocation and slightly softens the logical friction of the third-person pronoun 'they' applying to 'your own evil deeds' (since 'committed' can more easily be read as a passive-leaning or general action in this context).

revisions.from: hand us over into the hand
revisions.to: deliver us into the hand
revisions.rationale: Using 'hand' twice in close proximity creates an awkward English repetition that is not present in the Hebrew (which uses תֵּת, 'give/deliver', and יַד, 'hand'). Changing the verb to 'deliver' resolves the clunkiness while preserving the Hebrew idiom.

revisions.from: Beth-shemesh that is in the land of Egypt
revisions.to: Beth-shemesh, which is in the land of Egypt,
revisions.rationale: In English, proper nouns typically take non-restrictive relative clauses ('which') rather than restrictive ones ('that'), unless preceded by a definite article (e.g., 'the Beth-shemesh that is...'). Using 'which is' (or simply 'Beth-shemesh in the land of Egypt') is more grammatically standard.

revisions.from: because backsliding Israel had committed adultery on every such account
revisions.to: because of all the times backsliding Israel had committed adultery
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'on every such account' is awkward and syntactically appears to modify 'committed adultery' rather than 'sent her away.' Rendering the compound conjunction (עַל כָּל אֹדוֹת אֲשֶׁר) as 'because of all the times' or 'for all the adulteries' is much more natural in English while preserving the comprehensive force of 'כָּל'.

revisions.from: anguish like a woman in labor
revisions.to: anguish like that of a woman in labor
revisions.rationale: Adding 'that of' resolves the slight grammatical mismatch of comparing an emotion ('anguish') directly to a person ('a woman'), making the English comparison more precise while maintaining the optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: bring agitation
revisions.to: bring unrest
revisions.rationale: "Bring unrest" provides a much sharper and more natural English contrast to "give rest" in the preceding clause, capturing the rhetorical contrast of the Hebrew (הִרְגִּיעַ vs הִרְגִּיז) more effectively and idiomatically than "bring agitation."

revisions.from: and on the hill country
revisions.to: and in the hill country
revisions.rationale: In English, the standard idiom is 'in the hill country' rather than 'on the hill country.' The Hebrew preposition בְּ (in/on/at) naturally translates to 'in' when referring to a regional expanse like the hill country of Ephraim.

revisions.from: she will never again be inhabited forever
revisions.to: she will never be inhabited again
revisions.rationale: Combining 'never again' and 'forever' is redundant and awkward in English. Rendering the phrase as 'she will never be inhabited again' or 'she will be inhabited no more forever' resolves the clash while preserving the emphatic permanence of the Hebrew (לֹא...עוֹד לָנֶצַח).

revisions.from: none returns empty-handed
revisions.to: none returns empty
revisions.rationale: Because the draft applies this clause to the arrows ('none [of the arrows] returns...'), adding '-handed' personifies the arrows in a way that is awkward in English. In biblical idiom (e.g., 2 Sam 1:22), weapons returning 'empty' (reqam) means they did not shed blood or find their mark. 'Empty' preserves the martial metaphor perfectly without the anatomical mismatch.

revisions.from: the time of vengeance for the LORD
revisions.to: the LORD's time of vengeance
revisions.rationale: The phrasing 'vengeance for the LORD' can ambiguously sound as though someone else is taking vengeance on His behalf. Rendering the lamed of possession as 'the LORD's time of vengeance' (or 'a time of vengeance belonging to the LORD') clarifies that He is the one executing it, which aligns perfectly with the following clause.

revisions.from: a multitude of waters is in the heavens
revisions.to: there is a multitude of waters in the heavens
revisions.rationale: Adding the expletive 'there is' makes the English clause much more natural and idiomatic while accurately reflecting the Hebrew verbless clause.

revisions.from: its rider
revisions.to: its driver
revisions.rationale: While 'rider' accurately reflects the Hebrew root רכב, in English one typically 'drives' a chariot rather than 'rides' it. 'Driver' or 'charioteer' would be more natural here, though 'rider' is defensible for consistency with the first half of the verse.

revisions.from: with the roar of its waves
revisions.to: with its tumultuous waves
revisions.rationale: Translating 'הֲמוֹן' purely as the auditory 'roar' creates a mixed metaphor in English, as one cannot physically be 'covered' by a sound. Rendering it as 'tumultuous waves' or 'the multitude of its waves' correctly captures both the chaotic noise and the physical mass covering the city.

revisions.from: widowed from their God, from the LORD of Armies
revisions.to: left as widows by their God, by the LORD of Armies (or 'bereft of their God, of the LORD of Armies')
revisions.rationale: In English, a person is not typically 'widowed from' someone; they are simply 'widowed' or 'bereft of' them. While 'widowed' excellently captures the Hebrew metaphor (אַלְמָן), combining it with 'from' creates an unnatural collocation. Using 'left as widows by' or 'bereft of' maintains the imagery while using natural English idiom.

revisions.from: O destroying mountain, declares the LORD, which destroys
revisions.to: O destroying mountain, declares the LORD, you who destroy
revisions.rationale: Placing 'declares the LORD' between 'mountain' and 'which' creates an awkward separation in English. Changing 'which destroys' to 'you who destroy' clarifies that the mountain is being directly addressed and resolves the dangling relative clause.

revisions.from: who led his glorious arm
revisions.to: who caused his glorious arm to go
revisions.rationale: In English, 'leading an arm' is an awkward collocation. The Hiphil participle of הלך ('to cause to go') with 'arm' as the object is better rendered as 'caused his glorious arm to go' or 'sent his glorious arm' to preserve the imagery naturally.

revisions.from: is near to come
revisions.to: is soon to come / is near, about to come
revisions.rationale: "Near to come" is an archaic calque of the Hebrew infinitive construct (קָרוֹב ... לָבוֹא). In modern English, "is soon to come" or "is near, about to come" is much more natural while preserving the imminence of the original.

revisions.from: is hurrying very quickly
revisions.to: hastens swiftly / is approaching very quickly
revisions.rationale: While "hurrying" accurately reflects the Piel verb (מִהֲרָה), in modern English "hurrying" is typically reserved for animate subjects. "Hastens swiftly" (as noted in the footnote) or "is approaching very quickly" sounds more natural for an impending calamity.

revisions.from: and this city will be burned with fire.
revisions.to: and you will burn this city with fire.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verb תִּשְׂרֹף is Qal active 2nd person masculine singular, and 'this city' is preceded by the direct object marker (אֶת). The text means Zedekiah himself will be responsible for burning the city. Translating it as a passive ignores the MT grammar and the direct object marker.

revisions.from: and the sound of the trumpet we will not hear, and for bread we will not be hungry
revisions.to: and we will not hear the sound of the trumpet, and we will not be hungry for bread
revisions.rationale: Fronting the objects and prepositional phrases mimics the Hebrew word order but results in awkward, unnatural English syntax. Reverting to standard English Subject-Verb-Object order improves readability without sacrificing accuracy.

revisions.from: spoke this disaster
revisions.to: pronounced this disaster
revisions.rationale: In English, one does not typically 'speak' a disaster. While 'spoke' is the literal rendering of דִּבֶּר, when the object is a calamity or judgment, 'pronounced' or 'decreed' is much more natural and accurately conveys the judicial force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: said privately to Gedaliah at Mizpah, saying,
revisions.to: spoke privately to Gedaliah at Mizpah, saying, [OR] said privately to Gedaliah at Mizpah,
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of 'said... saying' (amar... le'mor) is redundant and awkward in English. Changing the first verb to 'spoke' or omitting 'saying' improves the natural flow without losing any meaning.

revisions.from: cistern while the king was sitting at the Benjamin Gate.
revisions.to: cistern. Now the king was sitting at the Benjamin Gate.
revisions.rationale: Placing 'while the king was sitting...' at the end of the sentence syntactically attaches it to the action of putting Jeremiah into the cistern. In the Hebrew narrative, this disjunctive clause (waw + subject + participle) provides the circumstantial setting for Ebed-melech's hearing and his subsequent action of going to the king (v. 8), not the time when Jeremiah was thrown into the cistern. Breaking it into a new sentence or using a parenthetical clarifies the narrative timeline.

revisions.from: took Jeremiah the prophet to himself
revisions.to: had Jeremiah the prophet brought to him
revisions.rationale: While 'took' is the formal equivalent of לקח, the phrase 'took [someone] to himself' is an awkward English idiom that can imply taking possession or even marriage. In the context of a royal summons, 'had [him] brought to him' or 'brought [him] to him' accurately conveys the directive sense of the king summoning the prophet without sounding unnatural.

revisions.from: death—and what the king said to you,’
revisions.to: death; and tell us what the king said to you,’
revisions.rationale: Appending the second object ('and what the king said to you') after the intervening threat creates a disjointed English sentence. Supplying 'tell us' (or reordering the objects, though that sacrifices formal equivalence) resolves the awkwardness while preserving the Hebrew sequence.

revisions.from: all these words—from his mouth?
revisions.to: all these words? Was it from his mouth?
revisions.rationale: The em-dash creates a slightly awkward, disjointed question in English. Breaking it into two questions ('How did you write all these words? Was it from his mouth?') better captures the twofold nature of their inquiry regarding his method and source, which Baruch answers directly in the next verse.

revisions.from: cut it off with the scribe’s knife and throw it
revisions.to: cut them off with the scribe’s knife and throw them
revisions.rationale: The pronoun 'it' lacks a clear singular antecedent in the immediate English clause ('three or four columns'). While the Hebrew suffix on the verb 'cut' is singular (likely referring to the scroll, מְגִלָּה, as a whole), English grammar makes 'cut them off' (referring to the columns) much more natural and prevents pronoun confusion.

revisions.from: and all the officials.
revisions.to: and all the other officials.
revisions.rationale: Because 'all the officials' was already introduced before the list of names, repeating 'and all the officials' at the end is slightly awkward in English. Adding 'other' clarifies the meaning while maintaining the Hebrew's summarizing function, a common adjustment in optimal-equivalence translations.

revisions.from: They will yet say this word
revisions.to: They will again say this word
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'yet' typically means 'up to now' or 'nevertheless.' Using it to mean 'again' or 'once more' (translating עוֹד) sounds slightly archaic. 'Again' or 'once more' is clearer and more natural for contemporary readers while maintaining optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: each in his tent, they would rise up
revisions.to: they would rise up, each in his tent,
revisions.rationale: The major Masoretic disjunctive accent (athnach) falls on 'wounded men' (מְדֻקָּרִ֑ים), marking the end of the protasis. 'Each in his tent' (אִ֤ישׁ בְּאָהֳלוֹ֙) begins the apodosis and modifies their rising up. In English, placing 'each in his tent' before 'they would rise up' creates a slightly awkward absolute phrase that looks like it modifies the remaining men in the previous clause. Reordering it slightly improves the English flow while respecting the Hebrew phrasing.

revisions.from: the dungeon house
revisions.to: the dungeon
revisions.rationale: While 'dungeon house' literally reflects 'בֵּית הַבּוֹר' (house of the pit/cistern), it is slightly awkward in English since a dungeon is typically a subterranean room rather than a house. Rendering it simply as 'the dungeon' (or 'the house of the pit' if a more literal rendering is desired) might read more naturally, though the current rendering is defensible.

revisions.from: please let my plea fall before you
revisions.to: please let my plea come before you
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew idiom 'let my plea fall' means to present a petition or have it accepted. In English, 'fall before you' can sound like the plea is dropping to the ground and failing. It is better to use the idiomatic meaning in the text and place the literal Hebrew in the footnote.

revisions.from: there was a captain of the guard there
revisions.to: a captain of the guard was there
revisions.rationale: The repetition of 'there' ('there was... there') is slightly awkward in English. Rendering וְשָׁם (and there) as 'a captain of the guard was there' or simply 'there was a captain of the guard' improves the flow without losing any meaning.

revisions.from: But some of the poorest of the people, who had nothing, Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard left in the land of Judah
revisions.to: But Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard left some of the poorest of the people, who had nothing, in the land of Judah
revisions.rationale: The draft retains the Hebrew Object-Verb-Subject word order, which results in an awkward English sentence. Reordering to standard Subject-Verb-Object syntax produces much more natural English while fully preserving the meaning and optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: blinded Zedekiah’s eyes
revisions.to: put out Zedekiah’s eyes
revisions.rationale: In English, one typically 'blinds' a person, whereas one 'puts out' or 'gouges out' a person's eyes. Because the Hebrew explicitly makes 'eyes' the direct object, 'put out' is the more natural English idiom for this physical act.

revisions.from: just as he speaks to you
revisions.to: just as he tells you
revisions.rationale: While 'speaks' is a valid translation of יְדַבֵּר, 'speaks to you' is slightly unnatural in English when referring to giving instructions or making requests. 'Tells you' or 'says to you' is more idiomatic for this comparative clause.

revisions.from: Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard, Nebushazban the chief officer, Nergal-sharezer the chief magus, and all the chief officials of the king of Babylon sent word.
revisions.to: So Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard sent word, along with Nebushazban the chief officer, Nergal-sharezer the chief magus, and all the chief officials of the king of Babylon.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verb (וַיִּשְׁלַח) is singular and appears at the beginning of the verse. Translating it early in the sentence avoids burying the verb at the very end of a long compound subject. Adding 'So' or 'Then' also better reflects the waw-consecutive, connecting their action to the king's prior command.

revisions.from: the New Gate of the LORD
revisions.to: the LORD's New Gate
revisions.rationale: While literally 'the new gate of the LORD', the phrasing 'the New Gate of the LORD' is slightly awkward in English. Rendering it as 'the LORD's New Gate' is more natural, or alternatively, supplying 'house' from the immediate context ('the New Gate of the LORD's house') clarifies that it is a gate of the temple complex.

revisions.from: has been to me a cause of my anger and my wrath
revisions.to: has been a cause of anger and wrath to me
revisions.rationale: The combination of the dative 'to me' alongside the possessive pronouns 'my anger' and 'my wrath' creates a redundant and clunky English phrase. Adjusting to 'a cause of anger and wrath to me' or 'has provoked my anger and my wrath' improves readability while preserving the source's meaning.

revisions.from: that I will not turn away from them, to do them good
revisions.to: that I will not turn away from doing good to them
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing with the comma can read awkwardly or ambiguously as a purpose clause ('in order to do them good'). Connecting the infinitive directly to the action ('turn away from doing good to them') better captures the Hebrew idiom, clarifying that God will never cease doing good to them.

revisions.from: open upon
revisions.to: open to
revisions.rationale: While 'open upon' is a literal rendering of the Hebrew preposition עַל (al), it sounds slightly archaic or awkward in contemporary English. 'Open to' is the standard English idiom for this expression and maintains the exact meaning.

revisions.from: that I am speaking concerning them
revisions.to: that I am promising them
revisions.rationale: While 'speaking concerning them' is a literal rendering of דֹּבֵר עֲלֵיהֶם, 'promising them' is the standard English idiom for this prophetic formula and better captures the performative nature of God's speech here. Consider swapping the main text and footnote b to improve readability without losing the underlying meaning.

revisions.from: and his mouth will speak with his mouth, and his eyes will see his eyes.
revisions.to: and he will speak with him mouth to mouth, and his eyes will see his eyes.
revisions.rationale: The strictly literal rendering 'his mouth will speak with his mouth' is overly wooden and ambiguous in English, sounding as if a person's own mouth is talking to itself. Rendering the syntax as 'he will speak with him mouth to mouth' (or 'face to face') resolves the pronoun ambiguity while preserving the concrete imagery.

revisions.from: deeds will be written in a scroll
revisions.to: deeds will be written
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew סֵפֶר refers to the legal document itself. By supplying 'deeds' as the subject and translating סֵפֶר as 'scroll,' the draft creates an awkward redundancy implying the deed is written *inside* a scroll, rather than the scroll *being* the deed. Rendering it simply as 'deeds will be written' (or 'deeds will be signed') avoids this.

revisions.from: listen to receive correction
revisions.to: listen and receive correction
revisions.rationale: Translating the infinitive construct לָקַחַת (laqachat) literally as 'to receive' creates a slightly awkward English phrase ('listen to receive'). Rendering it as 'listen and receive' or 'listen or receive' is more natural in English and accurately reflects the consecutive or epexegetical function of the Hebrew infinitive here.

revisions.from: shall not drink wine forever
revisions.to: shall never drink wine / shall drink no wine forever
revisions.rationale: In English, the construction 'not [verb] forever' can inadvertently negate the duration rather than the action (i.e., 'you may drink wine, but not forever'). Rendering it as 'shall never drink wine' or 'shall drink no wine forever' ensures the absolute nature of the prohibition is clear.

revisions.from: bowls full of wine and cups
revisions.to: bowls full of wine, and cups
revisions.rationale: Without a comma, the English phrasing can be momentarily misread as bowls containing both wine and cups. Adding a comma clarifies that he set out bowls of wine, as well as cups.

revisions.from: and vineyard and field and seed have not belonged to us.
revisions.to: and we have no vineyard, field, or seed.
revisions.rationale: The verb יִהְיֶה (yiqtol) indicates a present or ongoing state ('we do not have' / 'there is not to us'), not a present perfect ('have not belonged'). Additionally, rendering the Hebrew possession idiom (ל + היה) literally as '[objects] do not belong to us' with bare singular nouns is extremely awkward in English; 'we have no [objects]' is the standard and more natural equivalent.

revisions.from: turned back and brought back
revisions.to: turned around and brought back / changed their minds and brought back
revisions.rationale: In English, 'turned back' can imply physical retreat, and pairing it with 'brought back' creates an awkward repetition. 'Turned around' or 'changed their minds' better captures the reversal of their decision and improves the English flow.

revisions.from: forced them into slavery as male and female slaves
revisions.to: subjugated them as male and female slaves / forced them to be male and female slaves
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'forced them into slavery as... slaves' is slightly redundant. Translating the verb (כבש) as 'subjugated' or 'forced them to be' avoids the repetition while accurately reflecting the coercive force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: which has gone up away from you
revisions.to: which has withdrawn from you
revisions.rationale: "Gone up away from you" is an overly literal rendering of the Hebrew idiom (עָלָה מֵעַל) that results in clunky English. "Withdrawn from you" accurately conveys the contextual meaning of lifting a siege and is the standard rendering in optimal-equivalence translations. The literal phrasing is better suited for the footnote.

revisions.from: cities, saying,
revisions.to: cities:
revisions.rationale: Because of the long intervening circumstantial clause ('when Nebuchadnezzar...'), placing 'saying' at the very end of the sentence creates a dangling modifier in English, making it sound as though the cities or the Babylonian army are speaking. Replacing 'saying,' with a colon (or rendering it as 'He said:') correctly points forward to the divine speech without the awkward syntax.

revisions.from: each of you shall send away his Hebrew brother who has been sold to you
revisions.to: you shall each send away your Hebrew brother who has been sold to you
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a 2nd person plural verb with a distributive subject ('each') and a 3rd person suffix ('his brother'), followed by 2nd person singular suffixes ('to you'). In English, mixing 'each of you... his' with 'sold to you' creates an awkward pronoun clash. Translating as 'you shall each send away your Hebrew brother' harmonizes the pronouns naturally while accurately reflecting the 2nd person address.

revisions.from: had turned back today and had done
revisions.to: turned back today and did
revisions.rationale: In English, the past perfect tense ('had turned back') clashes awkwardly with the present temporal adverb 'today.' Changing the verbs to simple past ('turned back today and did') resolves the grammatical friction while preserving the literal translation of הַיּוֹם. Alternatively, if the past perfect is strongly desired to set up the contrast in verse 16, הַיּוֹם should be rendered as 'recently.'

revisions.from: and with the Levitical priests, my ministers.
revisions.to: and my covenant with the Levitical priests, my ministers.
revisions.rationale: Because of the intervening result clause ('so that he would not have a son...'), the prepositional phrase 'and with...' feels disconnected and could momentarily be misread as modifying 'reigning'. Supplying 'my covenant' (as many standard translations do) clarifies that this coordinates directly with 'my covenant with David'.

revisions.from: And she will be to me
revisions.to: And this city will be to me
revisions.rationale: The literal feminine pronoun refers back to Jerusalem (v. 4, 6). However, because the immediately preceding verses (vv. 7-8) focus on the people (plural), 'she' is jarring and its referent is unclear in English. Translating as 'this city' (and subsequently changing 'to her' to 'to it' at the end of the verse) clarifies the referent.

revisions.from: everyone who walks in them does not know peace
revisions.to: no one who walks in them knows peace
revisions.rationale: In English, the construction 'everyone... does not' is often ambiguous and can be misread as 'not everyone does' (meaning some do and some don't). Rendering it as 'no one who walks in them knows peace' or 'whoever walks in them does not know peace' is much more natural and removes the ambiguity while faithfully translating the Hebrew 'כֹּל... לֹא'.

revisions.from: for good
revisions.to: for their good
revisions.rationale: In modern English, the phrase 'for good' is most commonly used as an idiom meaning 'permanently.' While the context makes the benevolent intent clear, rendering it as 'for their good' (currently in the footnote) completely removes this ambiguity and clearly communicates the Hebrew לְטוֹבָה.

revisions.from: and one basket
revisions.to: and the other basket
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew repeats 'אֶחָד' (one) for both baskets (הַדּוּד אֶחָד ... וְהַדּוּד אֶחָד), standard English idiom for contrasting two specific items is 'One [item]... and the other [item]...'. Repeating 'one basket' feels slightly clunky.

revisions.from: the prophets, declares the LORD, who steal my words, each from his neighbor.
revisions.to: the prophets who steal my words, each from his neighbor, declares the LORD.
revisions.rationale: Placing 'declares the LORD' between 'the prophets' and the relative clause 'who steal...' awkwardly interrupts the noun phrase. Moving the prophetic formula to the end of the sentence (or immediately after 'Therefore') resolves this while preserving all elements.

revisions.from: take their own tongues
revisions.to: use their own tongues
revisions.rationale: Translating לָקַח (laqach) literally as 'take' in this context creates an awkward, almost grotesque physical image in English. The Hebrew idiom refers to employing or using their tongues to manufacture prophecies.

revisions.from: therefore this is what the LORD says:
revisions.to: then this is what the LORD says:
revisions.rationale: In English, a conditional 'if' clause is naturally followed by 'then' rather than 'therefore.' While 'therefore' is a literal rendering of לָכֵן (laken), 'then' provides a smoother transition into the apodosis without losing the logical force.

revisions.from: the people who say, “The burden of the LORD,” I will punish that man
revisions.to: one of the people who says, “The burden of the LORD,” I will punish that man
revisions.rationale: Translating 'the people who say' (plural) creates an awkward number clash with the singular 'that man' in the apodosis. Rendering it as 'one of the people who says' (or 'any of the people who says') resolves the English clash, accurately reflects the distributive singular of the Hebrew verb (יֹאמַר), and smoothly anticipates 'that man.'

revisions.from: cast you away, you and the city that I gave to you and to your fathers, from before my face.
revisions.to: cast you away from before my face, you and the city that I gave to you and to your fathers.
revisions.rationale: The long appositive phrase awkwardly interrupts the verb and its prepositional modifier ('cast you away... from before my face'). Moving the prepositional phrase immediately after the verb makes the English flow much more naturally.

revisions.from: make you to this people a fortified wall of bronze
revisions.to: make you a fortified wall of bronze to this people
revisions.rationale: While the current word order mirrors the Hebrew and is found in several formal translations, moving the prepositional phrase 'to this people' after the object complement ('a fortified wall of bronze') creates smoother, more natural English syntax without losing any formal equivalence.

revisions.from: the kings who sit for David on his throne
revisions.to: the kings who sit on David's throne
revisions.rationale: Translating the lamed as 'for' ('sit for David') is overly literal and creates an awkward English phrase that can imply substitution (acting as a placeholder) rather than dynastic succession. The lamed here functions to indicate possession or reference. 'who sit on David's throne' is the standard and more natural rendering of this Hebrew idiom. The current footnote 'a' can be adapted to explain the literal phrasing if desired.

revisions.from: All your shepherds the wind will shepherd away
revisions.to: The wind will shepherd away all your shepherds
revisions.rationale: The Object-Subject-Verb word order ('All your shepherds the wind will shepherd away') is somewhat clunky in English. Reordering to standard Subject-Verb-Object improves readability and natural flow while fully preserving the lexical choices, the wordplay, and the poetic imagery.

revisions.from: those before whom you are afraid
revisions.to: those of whom you are afraid
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew idiom יָגוֹר מִפְּנֵי means 'to be afraid of' or 'to dread.' Translating מִפְּנֵי literally as 'before' results in the awkward English phrase 'before whom you are afraid.' 'Of whom you are afraid' (or 'whom you dread') is standard English and accurately conveys the source meaning without losing any grammatical force.

revisions.from: make disappear from among them
revisions.to: remove from them / banish from among them
revisions.rationale: In standard English syntax, 'make disappear' requires the object to be placed between the two verbs (e.g., 'make the sound disappear'). Because the object here is a long list, keeping the verbs together before the object creates an awkward phrasing. Using a single transitive verb like 'remove', 'banish', or 'eliminate' resolves this while preserving the causative force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: drink, and stagger, and act
revisions.to: drink, stagger, and act
revisions.rationale: Standard English punctuation for a list of three verbs uses commas to separate the items, with a conjunction before the last item (A, B, and C). The current punctuation ('A, and B, and C') is slightly clunky. Alternatively, 'drink and stagger and act' without commas would also work to reflect the polysyndeton.

revisions.from: made all the nations drink, to whom the LORD had sent me.
revisions.to: made all the nations to whom the LORD had sent me drink.
revisions.rationale: In English, the relative clause 'to whom...' should immediately follow its antecedent ('all the nations') to avoid a misplaced modifier. (Adding 'it' at the end—'drink it'—is also a common and natural way to resolve the transitive verb in English).

revisions.from: will make even them serve
revisions.to: will enslave even them
revisions.rationale: "Make even them serve" is slightly clunky in English. Translating the idiom עבד ב as "enslave" or "make slaves of" is more natural and accurately captures the force of subjugation, while still reflecting the root concept of servitude.

revisions.from: are full, and your dispersions have come
revisions.to: are fulfilled, and your dispersions have come
revisions.rationale: In English, days or time periods are 'fulfilled' or 'completed,' not 'full.' Alternatively, you could coordinate the nouns to share the verb without supplying 'have come' (e.g., 'for the days of your slaughter and your dispersions are fulfilled'), which would be closer to the Hebrew syntax.

revisions.from: over which my name is called
revisions.to: that is called by my name
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering 'over which my name is called' is a Hebrew idiom denoting ownership or identification. In English, it sounds unnatural and its meaning is less transparent. 'That is called by my name' (or 'which bears my name') accurately conveys the idiom's meaning in natural English and is the standard way to render this phrase.

revisions.from: and for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon
revisions.to: and I am sending for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon
revisions.rationale: Because the verb 'taking' intervenes between 'sending for' and 'and for Nebuchadnezzar,' the preposition 'for' is left dangling and awkwardly appears to attach to 'taking' (i.e., 'taking... for Nebuchadnezzar'). Supplying the elided verb clarifies the syntax and improves readability.

revisions.from: these your cities
revisions.to: these cities of yours
revisions.rationale: "These your cities" is slightly archaic and clunky in modern English. "These cities of yours" flows more naturally while fully preserving the demonstrative and possessive force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: make them rejoice from their sorrow
revisions.to: make them rejoice instead of their sorrow
revisions.rationale: In English, 'rejoice from' can confusingly imply that sorrow is the source or cause of the joy. The Hebrew preposition מִן (min) here indicates separation or substitution ('away from', 'after', or 'instead of'). Rendering it as 'instead of' or 'after' clarifies the meaning while maintaining the contrast.

revisions.from: give up to prey
revisions.to: give as prey
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'give up to prey' is awkward in English. When 'prey' is used as a noun meaning spoil or booty, 'give as prey' or 'give up as prey' is much more natural. Alternatively, 'give as spoil' would also work well here.

revisions.from: you will come to Babylon
revisions.to: you will go to Babylon
revisions.rationale: Since the speaker (Jeremiah) is in Jerusalem, 'come to Babylon' is awkward in English. The Hebrew verb בּוֹא can mean 'go,' 'come,' or 'enter' depending on the deictic center. 'Go to Babylon' or 'enter Babylon' is more natural and accurate to the spatial perspective.

revisions.from: revealed my case
revisions.to: committed my case
revisions.rationale: In English, one does not typically 'reveal' a legal case or dispute. While the Hebrew verb 'galah' means to uncover or reveal, when paired with 'riv' (case/dispute), it idiomatically means to lay open, present, or commit a case to someone. 'Committed my case' or 'laid my case before you' provides a much more natural English collocation while preserving the legal metaphor.

revisions.from: I will let remain
revisions.to: I will let it remain
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a casus pendens (left-dislocation) construction with a resumptive pronoun on the verb (וְהִנַּחְתִּיו, 'I will leave it'). In English, fronting a long object clause before 'let remain' is slightly awkward without a resumptive pronoun. Adding 'it' smooths the English syntax and accurately reflects the Hebrew pronominal suffix.

revisions.from: the man
revisions.to: man / mankind
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses the definite article (הָאָדָם), it functions here as a generic article denoting a class. In English, using 'the man' strongly implies a specific individual rather than humanity as a whole. Dropping the article ('man') or using 'mankind' accurately conveys the generic singular of the Hebrew without misleading the English reader.

revisions.from: to emptiness. They have been made to stumble
revisions.to: to worthless things, which have made them stumble
revisions.rationale: While 'emptiness' is a faithful lexical gloss for שָׁוְא, 'burning incense to emptiness' is somewhat awkward in English, as incense is typically offered to an entity or object rather than an abstract void. Rendering it as 'worthless things' (or 'falsehood') provides a more natural English object and, crucially, provides a plural antecedent that allows you to retain the active, causative force of the Hebrew verb (וַיַּכְשִׁלוּם, 'and they made them stumble') without resorting to the passive voice.

revisions.from: repay double their iniquity and their sin
revisions.to: repay them double for their iniquity and their sin (or 'doubly repay their iniquity and their sin')
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'repay double their iniquity' is slightly awkward in English and could momentarily be misread as two verbs ('repay' and 'double'). Adding 'them... for' or using the adverb 'doubly' clarifies the syntax and improves the flow.

revisions.from: and have spoken in my name a lie that I did not command them.
revisions.to: and have spoken lying words in my name that I did not command them.
revisions.rationale: Translating דָבָר שֶׁקֶר (literally 'a word of falsehood') simply as 'a lie' creates awkward English syntax ('spoken in my name a lie') and makes the relative clause slightly unnatural, as one does not typically 'command a lie.' Rendering it as 'lying words' smooths the syntax, accounts for the noun דָבָר, and pairs better with the verb 'command'.

revisions.from: you shall say, saying,
revisions.to: you shall speak, saying, (or simply "you shall say:")
revisions.rationale: Translating both the finite verb and the infinitive construct of אמר as 'say, saying' creates an awkward redundancy in English. In optimal-equivalence translations, it is standard to render this combination as 'speak, saying,' 'say as follows,' or simply 'say:' to introduce the quotation.

revisions.from: from off
revisions.to: from
revisions.rationale: "From off" is an archaic, overly literal rendering of the compound preposition מֵעַל. In modern English, "from" (or "off") is more natural and conveys the exact same meaning without sounding dated.

revisions.from: say to Hananiah, saying,
revisions.to: say to Hananiah,
revisions.rationale: Translating לֵאמֹר as 'saying' immediately after the verb 'say' creates a redundant and awkward English construction. The colon and quotation marks sufficiently serve the function of introducing the direct speech.

revisions.from: said to the officials and to all the people, saying,
revisions.to: said to the officials and to all the people,
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the Hebrew quotative frame 'said... saying' (וַיֹּאמְרוּ ... לֵאמֹר) results in redundant and awkward English. Dropping 'saying' (or changing 'said' to 'spoke') improves the flow while maintaining optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: A sentence of death is for this man
revisions.to: This man deserves a sentence of death
revisions.rationale: The verbless Hebrew clause (מִשְׁפַּט מָוֶת לָאִישׁ הַזֶּה) is rendered somewhat woodenly by supplying 'is'. Rendering it by translating the idiom dynamically ('This man deserves a sentence of death') or as an exclamation ('A death sentence for this man!') is much more natural in English legal/narrative contexts.

revisions.from: When King Jehoiakim, and all his warriors, and all the officials heard his words,
revisions.to: When King Jehoiakim, all his warriors, and all the officials heard his words,
revisions.rationale: The comma after 'Jehoiakim' followed immediately by 'and' creates an awkward list structure. Adjusting the conjunctions or commas makes the English flow much more naturally.

revisions.from: said to all the assembly of the people, saying,
revisions.to: spoke to all the assembly of the people, saying,
revisions.rationale: Rendering both the finite verb (אמר) and the infinitive construct (לאמר) with the root 'say' creates an awkward redundancy in English ('said... saying'). Using 'spoke' for the finite verb resolves this while preserving the Hebrew discourse structure.

revisions.from: Then men from the elders
revisions.to: Then some men from the elders
revisions.rationale: 'Men from the elders' is slightly abrupt in English. Adding 'some' (as noted in the footnote) provides the expected quantifier for an indefinite plural subject in a partitive construction, improving naturalness.

revisions.from: Elnathan son of Achbor, and men with him, to Egypt.
revisions.to: Elnathan son of Achbor and men with him.
revisions.rationale: Repeating 'to Egypt' at the end of the appositive phrase creates awkward, dangling English syntax. While it reflects a resumptive repetition in the Hebrew, optimal equivalence typically drops or restructures such redundancies to maintain a natural English flow.

revisions.from: and he said to all the people of Judah, saying,
revisions.to: and he spoke to all the people of Judah, saying,
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering 'said... saying' (translating the Hebrew idiom וַיֹּאמֶר ... לֵאמֹר) is redundant and awkward in English. Changing 'said' to 'spoke' (or simply omitting 'saying,') resolves the redundancy while maintaining the formal introduction to the quotation.

revisions.from: make your ways and your deeds good
revisions.to: amend your ways and your deeds
revisions.rationale: While 'make good' reflects the literal Hiphil morphology of יטב, 'make your ways good' is not a natural English collocation. 'Amend your ways' is the standard, idiomatic English equivalent that perfectly captures the causative/corrective force without sounding wooden.

revisions.from: they will bury
revisions.to: they will bury the dead
revisions.rationale: In English, the verb 'bury' typically expects a direct object. Supplying 'the dead' (or 'them') smooths the English syntax without altering the meaning, though leaving it implicit as in the Hebrew is defensible.

revisions.from: stiffened their neck
revisions.to: stiffened their necks
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses a collective singular for 'neck' (עָרְפָּם), standard English grammar typically pluralizes body parts when the subject is plural ('they'). If updated to 'necks', the footnote could then retain the literal gloss: 'Hebrew *their neck*, an idiom...'

revisions.from: famine into the hand
revisions.to: famine, into the hand
revisions.rationale: Because the direct object of 'I will give' is exceptionally long (spanning 25 words), the lack of a comma before the prepositional phrase 'into the hand of' creates a garden-path reading ('from the famine into the hand'). Adding a comma clarifies the syntactic boundary.

revisions.from: —oracle of the LORD—
revisions.to: , declares the LORD,
revisions.rationale: While 'oracle of the LORD' is a faithful formal rendering of the noun phrase נְאֻם יְהוָה, inserting it as a parenthetical interjection can feel slightly disjointed in English flow. 'declares the LORD' provides a smoother reading while maintaining the prophetic formula, though the current rendering is defensible.

revisions.from: because you bore me
revisions.to: that you bore me
revisions.rationale: In English laments (e.g., 'Woe to me...'), 'that' is the standard idiomatic conjunction to introduce the cause of distress. 'Because' is grammatically defensible but feels slightly prosaic and disrupts the poetic cadence.

revisions.from: I bereaved, I destroyed my people
revisions.to: I bereaved them, I destroyed my people
revisions.rationale: In English, the verb 'bereave' strongly expects an immediate object, and often specifies what is lost (e.g., 'of children', which is the primary sense of the Piel of שׁכל). Stacking the verbs with a delayed object ('I bereaved, I destroyed my people') is syntactically awkward. Adding a pronoun ('I bereaved them') or translating the specific nuance ('I made them childless') improves readability while maintaining the force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: and bronze
revisions.to: or bronze
revisions.rationale: In an English rhetorical question expecting a negative answer, 'or' is typically more natural than 'and' for coordinating the objects, preventing the sentence from feeling like a run-on.

revisions.from: And the remnant of them I will give to the sword
revisions.to: And I will give their remnant to the sword
revisions.rationale: The object-first word order ('the remnant of them I will give') is slightly clunky in English. Standardizing the word order to 'I will give their remnant' improves readability without losing meaning.

revisions.from: So I went to Perath, and dug, and took
revisions.to: So I went to Perath, dug, and took
revisions.rationale: In English, a series of three verbs is typically rendered as a list (A, B, and C) rather than repeating the conjunction with commas (A, and B, and C). Removing the first 'and' improves the natural flow of the sentence while preserving the sequence of actions.

revisions.from: arise, go
revisions.to: arise and go
revisions.rationale: Adding 'and' between the two imperatives smooths out the English phrasing without losing the force of the Hebrew serial imperatives (וְקוּם לֵךְ).

revisions.from: Remember; do not break
revisions.to: Remember, do not break
revisions.rationale: A semicolon after a single imperative verb ('Remember') feels overly abrupt and disjointed in English. A comma (or adding 'and') provides a smoother transition to the following clause while maintaining the urgency of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: burnt offering and grain offering
revisions.to: a burnt offering and a grain offering
revisions.rationale: Adding the indefinite articles makes the English flow more naturally, as these are typically treated as count nouns in English rather than mass nouns.

revisions.from: nor did it come up upon my heart
revisions.to: nor did it come into my heart
revisions.rationale: While attempting to preserve the Hebrew idiom (עָלְתָה עַל־לִבִּי), 'come up upon my heart' is overly wooden and awkward in English. 'Come into my heart' (or 'arise in my heart') preserves the literal 'heart' and the theological nuance the drafter intended, while utilizing a much more natural English phrasing.

revisions.from: in every way
revisions.to: in all the way
revisions.rationale: In English, 'in every way' idiomatically means 'in every manner' or 'in all respects,' which obscures the spatial/ethical metaphor of walking on a path. Rendering it as 'in all the way' (or 'in all the ways') better preserves the Hebrew metaphor of a directed path (בְּכָל־הַדֶּרֶךְ) without the unintended English idiom.

revisions.from: The corpse
revisions.to: The corpses
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew noun נִבְלַת (corpse/carcass) is morphologically singular, it functions collectively here. In English, referring to the remains of an entire population as a singular 'corpse' sounds unnatural, as if the people share a single body. Rendering it as a plural ('corpses' or 'dead bodies') is standard practice for this verse and produces much better English while accurately conveying the collective Hebrew meaning. (The footnote can then be adjusted to note 'Hebrew: The corpse').

revisions.from: a slaughtering arrow
revisions.to: a deadly arrow
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'slaughtering arrow' is unnatural in English, as arrows are not typically described as 'slaughtering' (which implies butchery). 'Deadly arrow' captures the lethal force of the Hebrew active participle (Kethiv) while remaining idiomatic.

revisions.from: the child from outside, the young men from the streets
revisions.to: children from the streets, young men from the squares
revisions.rationale: The singular 'עוֹלָל' (child) functions collectively here in parallel with the plural 'בַּחוּרִים' (young men). Translating it as plural 'children' is more natural in English. Additionally, 'from outside' can sound like an adjectival phrase modifying 'child' rather than the location from which they are cut off; rendering 'חוּץ' as 'streets' and 'רְחֹבוֹת' as 'squares' provides better poetic parallelism and clarity.

revisions.from: For hear, women, the word of the LORD
revisions.to: Now hear, O women, the word of the LORD / For hear the word of the LORD, O women
revisions.rationale: Placing the bare vocative 'women' directly between the verb and its object feels slightly clunky in English. Adding 'O' or rearranging the phrase (e.g., moving the vocative to the end or using 'Now' for the initial particle) improves the natural flow and preserves the elevated prophetic register.

revisions.from: Each man, beware
revisions.to: Let each man beware
revisions.rationale: The phrasing 'Each man, beware' reads awkwardly as a vocative address. The Hebrew uses a distributive singular subject ('ish') with a plural imperative ('hishammeru'). 'Let each man beware' or 'Each of you, beware' expresses this syntax much more naturally in English.

revisions.from: which their fathers taught them.
revisions.to: as their fathers taught them.
revisions.rationale: Translating אֲשֶׁר (asher) as the relative pronoun 'which' immediately after 'the Baals' creates an awkward English phrasing that sounds as though the fathers taught the deities themselves. Rendering it as the conjunction 'as' clarifies that the fathers taught them this behavior or practice of worship, which reads much more naturally in English.

revisions.from: turns in his own course
revisions.to: turns to his own course
revisions.rationale: In English, 'turns in his own course' can imply changing direction while already on a path. 'Turns to his own course' (or 'turns back to his own course') is more idiomatic and better conveys the intended meaning of returning to their headlong rush into sin, even though the Hebrew preposition is בְּ (in).

revisions.from: had done an abomination
revisions.to: had committed an abomination
revisions.rationale: In English, the standard collocation with 'abomination' is 'commit' rather than 'do.' While 'done' is a literal rendering of עָשׂוּ, 'committed' is much more natural and maintains the optimal-equivalence philosophy without losing any accuracy.

revisions.from: Why has this people turned away—Jerusalem—with a perpetual turning away?
revisions.to: Why has this people, Jerusalem, turned away with a perpetual turning away?
revisions.rationale: Placing 'Jerusalem' between dashes after the verb separates the appositive from the noun phrase it modifies ('this people'). Placing it immediately after 'this people' is more natural in English and better reflects the Hebrew syntax, where 'Jerusalem' immediately follows 'this people'.

revisions.from: hear my words
revisions.to: listen to my words
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'refused to hear' is slightly awkward because hearing is typically an involuntary auditory function. 'Refused to listen to' better captures the volitional rebellion and active disobedience implied by מֵאֲנוּ לִשְׁמֹעַ.

revisions.from: and to this day
revisions.to: even to this day
revisions.rationale: Following 'in the day,' the phrase 'and to this day' can sound like two disjointed points in time rather than a continuous span. Using 'even to this day' or 'until this day' (as noted in your lexical alternatives) better captures the continuous duration implied by the Hebrew idiom and the accompanying ongoing action ('rising early and warning').

revisions.from: which they have done to themselves
revisions.to: which they have brought upon themselves
revisions.rationale: While 'done to themselves' attempts to capture the disadvantageous force of לָהֶם (dativus incommodi), it pairs awkwardly with 'evil' (one typically 'commits evil' or 'brings evil upon' oneself). 'Brought upon themselves' or 'committed to their own hurt' is more idiomatic in English while preserving the reflexive/self-harming nuance.

revisions.from: no remnant will remain for them
revisions.to: no remnant will be left to them
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'remnant will remain' is slightly redundant in English. Rendering the verb היה (to be) as 'will be left' or simply 'they will have no remnant' avoids this repetition while accurately conveying the Hebrew idiom.

revisions.from: out from the land of Egypt
revisions.to: out of the land of Egypt
revisions.rationale: While 'out from' is technically accurate, 'out of the land of Egypt' is the standard, more idiomatic English phrasing for this frequent biblical idiom.

revisions.from: and as numerous as the streets of Jerusalem you have set up altars to Shame
revisions.to: and you have set up altars to Shame as numerous as the streets of Jerusalem
revisions.rationale: The current word order awkwardly places the comparative phrase before the subject and verb, making it sound like an adverbial modifier of 'set up.' Moving it after 'altars to Shame' clarifies that it modifies the altars, resulting in much more natural English syntax.

revisions.from: declares the LORD—steadfast love and compassion.
revisions.to: declares the LORD—my steadfast love and compassion.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses the definite article for 'steadfast love' and 'compassion' (הַחֶסֶד וְאֶת הָרַחֲמִים), which in this context refers specifically to God's own attributes (appositional to 'my peace'). Adding 'my' smooths the slightly jarring apposition across the prophetic formula and accurately reflects the definite/possessive force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: onto a land
revisions.to: to a land
revisions.rationale: While 'onto' attempts to capture the literal force of the preposition עַל (upon), 'hurl you... onto a land' is slightly awkward in English. 'To a land' or 'into a land' is much more idiomatic while still conveying the destination of the hurling, and it pairs naturally with 'from this land'.

revisions.from: in his end
revisions.to: at his end
revisions.rationale: "At his end" is more idiomatic in English than "in his end" when referring to the conclusion of someone's life, while still accurately reflecting the Hebrew preposition and suffix.

revisions.from: But they did not listen, and they did not incline their ear, but stiffened their neck
revisions.to: Yet they did not listen, and they did not incline their ear, but stiffened their neck
revisions.rationale: Using 'But' at the beginning of the sentence and 'but' again to introduce the contrasting clause creates a slightly clunky repetition in English. Changing the initial conjunction to 'Yet' (or changing the second to 'instead') improves the sentence's flow while preserving the contrast with the preceding verse.

revisions.from: My mountain in the field—your wealth, all your treasures, I will give as plunder,
revisions.to: My mountain in the field, your wealth, and all your treasures, I will give as plunder,
revisions.rationale: The em-dash obscures the intended syntax. Because the drafter explicitly chose to treat 'My mountain' as a fronted direct object rather than a vocative, the em-dash makes 'your wealth' read like an appositive defining the mountain. Using commas clarifies that these are all items in a list being given over as plunder.

revisions.from: the work of a craftsman's hands with an axe.
revisions.to: fashioned by a craftsman's hands with an axe.
revisions.rationale: In English, appending 'with an axe' directly after 'a craftsman's hands' creates a slightly clunky modification, as it lacks a clear verbal idea to attach to (unlike the Hebrew construct 'ma'aseh', which carries stronger verbal force). Supplying a participle like 'fashioned', 'worked', or 'shaped' clarifies the syntax and improves the flow without losing the source meaning.

revisions.from: But together they are
revisions.to: But they are altogether
revisions.rationale: In English, 'together' can misleadingly imply spatial proximity or joint action (e.g., 'when they gather, they are stupid'). 'Altogether' or 'all alike' more accurately and naturally conveys the intended Hebrew sense (וּבְאַחַת) of total or collective foolishness.

revisions.from: Topheth and the Valley
revisions.to: Topheth or the Valley
revisions.rationale: In English, when a list of alternatives is negated ('no longer be called'), 'or' is typically used instead of 'and' to clarify that neither name will apply, rather than just the combination of the two.

revisions.from: my soul would not be toward this people
revisions.to: my heart would not be toward this people
revisions.rationale: While 'soul' is the standard gloss for נֶפֶשׁ, attributing a 'soul' to God that 'is not toward' someone is highly unnatural in English and obscures the meaning of will or desire. 'Heart' better captures the anthropomorphic intent of disposition in English without losing the literal flavor. Consider swapping the main text with the footnote.

revisions.from: Is my inheritance to me a speckled bird of prey?
revisions.to: Is my inheritance a speckled bird of prey to me?
revisions.rationale: Placing 'to me' immediately after 'my inheritance' is slightly awkward in English. Moving it to the end of the clause improves the natural flow while retaining the dative force of the Hebrew (נַחֲלָתִי לִי).

revisions.from: Even I myself, look, I have seen it
revisions.to: Look, I myself have seen it
revisions.rationale: The string of emphatic markers ('Even I myself, look') is disjointed and awkward in English. 'Look, I myself have seen it' or 'I, too, have seen it' captures the Hebrew emphasis (גַּם אָנֹכִי הִנֵּה) smoothly without overloading the English syntax.

revisions.from: The temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD are these.
revisions.to: This is the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD.
revisions.rationale: Placing 'are these' at the end of the sentence mimics Hebrew word order but results in archaic, Yoda-like English syntax. While the Hebrew pronoun (הֵמָּה) is plural (likely referring to the multiple buildings of the temple complex), 'temple' is singular in English, making 'This is the temple...' the most natural rendering. (Alternatively, 'These are the temple...' if you wish to preserve the plural demonstrative).

revisions.from: and they do not hear
revisions.to: and no one hears
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew third-person plural verb (שָׁמְעוּ) is used impersonally here to mean 'one does not hear' or 'is not heard'. Translating it literally as 'they do not hear' creates a confusing English sentence where 'they' appears to refer back to the mountains or the pastures.

revisions.from: make mourning as for an only son for yourself
revisions.to: make mourning as for an only son
revisions.rationale: Placing 'for yourself' after the long phrase 'mourning as for an only son' creates an awkward, clunky English construction. The Hebrew 'לָךְ' (for yourself) functions as an ethical dative here; it is often best absorbed into the verb in English, or if retained, placed immediately after the verb ('make for yourself mourning...'). Omitting it yields the most natural reading.

revisions.from: from being a shepherd after you
revisions.to: from following you as a shepherd
revisions.rationale: "Being a shepherd after you" is a wooden rendering of 'מֵרֹעֶה אַחֲרֶיךָ' that sounds unnatural in English. "Following you as a shepherd" preserves both the shepherd metaphor and the directional meaning of 'after you' in a much more idiomatic way.

revisions.from: ; you know.
revisions.to: . You yourself know.
revisions.rationale: Appending "; you know." to the end of the previous clause makes it sound like a modern conversational filler. Separating it into its own sentence and adding "yourself" (reflecting the explicit pronoun אַתָּה) gives the phrase the appropriate solemn, emphatic weight.

revisions.from: too full
revisions.to: too strong
revisions.rationale: While 'full' is a literal rendering of מָלֵא, describing a wind as 'too full' is awkward in English and obscures the meaning of a high-velocity or forceful wind. 'Too strong' accurately conveys the meteorological force intended in this context and pairs naturally with 'for these'.

revisions.from: gird up your loins, and arise and speak
revisions.to: gird up your loins, arise, and speak
revisions.rationale: Removing the first 'and' improves the flow of the English sentence, avoiding a clunky 'and... and' sequence while maintaining the force of the consecutive verbs.

revisions.from: until the completion of
revisions.to: until the end of
revisions.rationale: 'Until the end of the eleventh year' is much more natural in English regnal dating formulas than 'until the completion of,' while still accurately translating תֹּם (tom).

revisions.from: their ear is uncircumcised, and they are not able to listen.
revisions.to: their ears are uncircumcised, and they are not able to listen.
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses a singular noun with a plural suffix (אָזְנָם), rendering it as a singular 'ear' in English creates a slight number mismatch with the immediately following plural pronoun ('they are not able'). Using the plural 'ears' is standard English idiom for collective body parts and smooths the transition without losing the underlying metaphor.

revisions.from: among my people, a fortress,
revisions.to: among my people—a fortress—
revisions.rationale: Using commas makes 'a fortress' look like it might be in apposition to 'my people.' Em-dashes clarify that 'a fortress' refers back to the role given to Jeremiah (cf. Jer 1:18) rather than describing the people.

revisions.from: pain like a woman giving birth
revisions.to: pain like that of a woman giving birth
revisions.rationale: Strictly speaking, 'pain like a woman' is a mismatched comparison in English (comparing 'pain' to 'a woman'). Adding 'that of' or 'as of' resolves the grammatical mismatch while preserving the vivid imagery of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: But shame has devoured the labor of our fathers from our youth
revisions.to: But from our youth, shame has devoured the labor of our fathers
revisions.rationale: Placing 'from our youth' at the beginning of the clause prevents it from awkwardly attaching to 'our fathers' in English, clarifying that it modifies the duration of the devouring rather than the fathers themselves.

revisions.from: the evil things
revisions.to: evil things
revisions.rationale: Translating the Hebrew article literally as 'the evil things' results in slightly clunky English here, as it sounds like a reference to specific previously mentioned items rather than a general category of behavior. 'Evil things' or 'evil deeds' is more natural.

revisions.from: Declare it to the nations: ‘Look, proclaim it concerning Jerusalem: Watchers
revisions.to: Declare it to the nations: ‘Look!’ Proclaim it concerning Jerusalem: ‘Watchers
revisions.rationale: The current punctuation places the second imperative ('proclaim it') inside the quotation addressed to the nations. In Hebrew, 'declare' (הַזְכִּירוּ) and 'proclaim' (הַשְׁמִיעוּ) are parallel plural imperatives addressed to the same heralds. The punctuation should separate them so it does not read as though the nations are being commanded to proclaim.

revisions.from: the heart of the king and the heart of the officials will perish
revisions.to: the heart of the king and the heart of the officials will fail
revisions.rationale: While 'perish' is the standard gloss for אבד, in English 'a heart perishing' sounds unnatural and obscures the idiom (loss of courage or reason). 'Will fail' is a natural English collocation with 'heart' that accurately conveys the Hebrew meaning while retaining the literal noun.

revisions.from: rule by their authority
revisions.to: rule at their direction
revisions.rationale: In English, 'the priests rule by their authority' strongly implies that the priests are ruling by their *own* authority (a common interpretation, found in the NIV/NASB/CSB). However, the lexical decision notes state the intention is to show the priests ruling under the influence of the prophets. To clearly communicate this intended meaning without pronoun ambiguity, 'rule at their direction' or 'rule by the prophets' authority' would be much clearer.

revisions.from: has had
revisions.to: has
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew idiom הָיָה + ל (to have) in a stative sense is best rendered with the simple present 'has' in English. 'Has had' is slightly awkward and over-translates the qatal form, which here simply describes their current, settled condition.

revisions.from: the good
revisions.to: good
revisions.rationale: In English, 'withheld good from you' is a more natural idiom than 'withheld the good,' which can sound like a philosophical category rather than the intended collective sense of blessing or prosperity. Dropping the article in English often better captures the Hebrew generic/collective article here.

revisions.from: through a land where no man passed through
revisions.to: through a land where no man passed
revisions.rationale: The repetition of 'through' ('through a land where... passed through') is slightly awkward and redundant. Dropping the second 'through' (or changing the relative pronoun to 'that no man passed through') improves the English flow while accurately conveying the Hebrew relative clause.

revisions.from: eat its fruit and its goodness
revisions.to: eat its fruit and its bounty
revisions.rationale: In English, one does not typically 'eat goodness.' While 'goodness' is a literal rendering of טוּב, 'bounty' or 'good things' pairs much more naturally with the verb 'eat' without losing the theological weight of God's provision.

revisions.from: turned for me into
revisions.to: turned before me into
revisions.rationale: In English, 'turned for me' can awkwardly imply doing a favor or acting on someone's behalf. Rendering the relational dative (לִי) as 'before me' or 'become to me' avoids this unintended positive connotation while preserving the personal, relational impact of the transformation.

revisions.from: know what you have done— a swift young she-camel,
revisions.to: know what you have done, you swift young she-camel,
revisions.rationale: Using a dash immediately after 'what you have done' makes the noun phrase 'a swift young she-camel' look like an appositive to the action rather than to the addressee ('you'). Adding 'you' or starting a new sentence ('You are a swift young she-camel...') clarifies the metaphor.

revisions.from: Keep your foot
revisions.to: Keep your feet
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew noun is singular, English idiomatically requires the plural 'feet' when referring to the state of going barefoot or unshod. Translating as singular sounds slightly unnatural in English.

revisions.from: the leg
revisions.to: your leg
revisions.rationale: In English, body parts typically take a possessive pronoun rather than a definite article in this context. Since 'your' was naturally supplied for 'train' (which also lacks a pronominal suffix in Hebrew), using 'your leg' improves both consistency and English idiom.

revisions.from: Stand now with your spells
revisions.to: Take your stand now with your spells
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'Stand now with' can sound slightly ambiguous in English (as if standing alongside someone). 'Take your stand with' or 'Persist in' better captures the Hebrew idiom of עמד + ב (to stand firm in, persist in, or rely upon) while maintaining the optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: and who fill mixed wine
revisions.to: and who fill cups with mixed wine
revisions.rationale: In English, one fills a container, not the liquid itself. The Hebrew Piel of מָלֵא with the accusative of material implies filling a vessel with that material. Adding 'cups with' or 'bowls with' produces natural English while preserving the meaning.

revisions.from: No more shall there be from there
revisions.to: No more shall there be in it
revisions.rationale: While מִשָּׁם literally means 'from there,' in English this sounds like spatial origin rather than location. In context (referring to Jerusalem in v. 19), it means 'in it' or 'there.' 'From there' is unnaturally wooden and obscures the meaning.

revisions.from: fill out his days
revisions.to: live out his days
revisions.rationale: 'Live out his days' is the standard English idiom for completing one's natural lifespan, whereas 'fill out his days' is slightly awkward and overly literal for מלא.

revisions.from: as rejoicing, and her people as joy
revisions.to: to be a joy, and her people a gladness
revisions.rationale: Translating the predicative accusative as 'as rejoicing' is awkward in English because 'rejoicing' typically functions as an uncountable action noun (a gerund), making it sound as though the city is being turned into an action rather than a source or state of joy. 'To be a joy' (or swapping with the footnote's 'for rejoicing') provides a much more natural English object complement while preserving the theological and grammatical force.

revisions.from: and broth of unclean things is in their vessels;
revisions.to: and whose vessels hold broth of unclean things;
revisions.rationale: The shift from the relative pronoun 'who' to an independent clause ('and broth... is...') breaks the descriptive chain. Using 'whose vessels hold' or 'and in whose vessels is' maintains the syntactic flow describing the rebellious people.

revisions.from: until he establishes and until he makes Jerusalem
revisions.to: until he establishes Jerusalem and makes it
revisions.rationale: The repetition of 'until he' separates the first transitive verb ('establishes') from its shared object ('Jerusalem'), leaving it hanging awkwardly. Supplying the object after the first verb (or combining the verbs under a single 'until he') creates a much more natural English flow.

revisions.from: in the earth
revisions.to: on the earth
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'in the earth' often implies being buried underground. 'On the earth' is the standard idiom for expressing global scope, which fits the universalizing context of Jerusalem's renown.

revisions.from: makes a guilt offering
revisions.to: is made a guilt offering
revisions.rationale: In English, 'makes an offering' usually refers to the person performing the ritual. Saying 'his life makes a guilt offering' sounds as though his life is the offerer rather than the offering itself. Adjusting to 'is made' or 'presents itself as' clarifies that his life is the sacrifice, even if the Hebrew verb is active.

revisions.from: and your garments like one who treads
revisions.to: and your garments like those of one who treads
revisions.rationale: In English, comparing 'garments' directly to 'one who treads' creates a mismatched comparison (comparing clothes to a person). Adding 'those of' clarifies that the garments are being compared to the garments of the wine-treader, accurately reflecting the Hebrew brachylogy (shortened comparison).

revisions.from: no one to support
revisions.to: no one to uphold me / no one to offer support
revisions.rationale: While 'help' functions well intransitively in English ('no one to help'), 'support' typically expects an object. Adding 'me' or phrasing it as 'offer support' smooths the English syntax while maintaining the parallel with the previous clause.

revisions.from: it will not be found there
revisions.to: none will be found there
revisions.rationale: The pronoun 'it' is ambiguous in English, potentially referring to the highway rather than the beasts. Translating the 3fs verb as 'none' or 'they' (referring to the beasts collectively) clarifies the meaning and reads more naturally.

revisions.from: they will say of you, “Ministers of our God.”
revisions.to: it will be said of you, “Ministers of our God.”
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'they will say of you' is slightly awkward as a dialogue tag here and unnecessarily converts the Niphal passive (יֵאָמֵר) into an active voice. Rendering it as 'it will be said of you' or 'you will be spoken of as' provides a smoother, more accurate parallel to the preceding passive clause ('you will be called').

revisions.from: to appoint for
revisions.to: to provide for
revisions.rationale: In English, the verb 'appoint' is transitive and sounds awkward without an immediate direct object. 'Provide for' functions well here, naturally anticipating the specific provisions (headdress, oil, garment) that follow the epexegetical 'to give them'.

revisions.from: drink deeply and delight yourselves in
revisions.to: drink deeply from and delight yourselves in
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew preposition מִן (from) governs both verbs. In English, one drinks 'from' a source but delights 'in' it. Combining them with only 'in' results in the slightly awkward phrase 'drink deeply in'. (If the lexical change to 'breast' is adopted, you might render it 'drink deeply with delight from her glorious breast' or 'drink deeply from her glorious breast and delight in it').

revisions.from: For as soon as Zion was in labor, she also gave birth to her children.
revisions.to: For as soon as Zion was in labor, she gave birth to her children.
revisions.rationale: When using the correlative 'as soon as' to capture the immediacy of the Hebrew construction, retaining 'also' (for גַּם) creates a slightly awkward and redundant English clause. Removing 'also' smooths the syntax while fully preserving the intended meaning.

revisions.from: we moan and moan
revisions.to: we moan continually
revisions.rationale: While 'moan and moan' accurately captures the intensifying/continuous force of the Hebrew infinitive absolute, it can sound slightly colloquial or repetitive for Isaiah's poetic register. 'Moan continually' or 'moan mournfully' might better elevate the tone while preserving the emphasis.

revisions.from: gird yourselves with
revisions.to: equip yourselves with
revisions.rationale: While 'gird' is the literal sense of אזר, one does not typically 'gird' firebrands, torches, or arrows in English (one girds a belt or a sword). 'Equip yourselves with' or 'surround yourselves with' conveys the intended meaning more naturally in English.

revisions.from: the sons of the foreigner
revisions.to: the foreigners / the sons of a foreigner
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses the definite article (הַנֵּכָר) to denote a class or category, in English 'the foreigner' sounds like a specific, previously mentioned individual. Rendering this as 'foreigners' (or 'sons of a foreigner' if the idiom must be retained) is much more natural and avoids misleading the reader.

revisions.from: I will give him
revisions.to: I will give each one
revisions.rationale: The drafter explicitly chose to retain the shift from plural to singular to reflect the Hebrew. However, a strict 'them... him' shift in a single English sentence often reads as a typographical error to modern readers. Rendering לוֹ as 'each one' or 'each of them' maintains the individualizing shift (from the collective to the individual) while respecting English syntax.

revisions.from: Foreigners’ sons
revisions.to: Foreigners / Sons of foreigners
