The adjective `הַשֵּׁנִית` (the second) modifies `הַחֹבֶרֶת` (the set/coupling), not the curtain itself. The current draft obscures the architecture of the tent.

revisions.from: bring the clasps into the loops
revisions.to: put the clasps into the loops
revisions.rationale: While the Hiphil of בוא often translates as 'bring,' in the context of fastening hardware (clasps into loops), 'put' or 'insert' is the standard English idiom. 'Bring' sounds unnatural here and implies movement toward a focal point rather than assembly.

revisions.from: you shall make them as eleven curtains
revisions.to: you shall make eleven curtains
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'you shall make them as eleven curtains' is slightly awkward in English. The Hebrew pronoun 'them' (אֹתָם) functions as the direct object, but in English, simply saying 'you shall make eleven curtains' is the most natural and standard way to render the clause without losing meaning.

revisions.from: the one frame, and two bases under the one frame
revisions.to: one frame, and two bases under the next frame
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew repetition of 'הָאֶחָד ... הָאֶחָד' is a standard distributive idiom meaning 'one ... another' or 'one ... the next.' Translating it literally as 'the one ... the one' produces awkward English and obscures the meaning, potentially implying four bases under a single frame.

revisions.from: with cherubim, the work of a skillful designer, you shall make them.
revisions.to: you shall make them with cherubim, the work of a skillful designer.
revisions.rationale: Fronting the phrase 'with cherubim' before the verb 'you shall make them' creates awkward, unnatural English syntax. Placing the verb first ('you shall make them with cherubim...') significantly improves readability while maintaining the exact meaning and optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear corners
revisions.to: for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear
revisions.rationale: Translating both מְקֻצְעֹת and יַרְכָתָיִם using the word 'corners' creates an awkward and redundant English phrase ('corners... corners'). Rendering יַרְכָתָיִם simply as 'at the rear' or 'in the back' accurately reflects the Hebrew and flows much better.

revisions.from: in the midst of
revisions.to: in the middle of
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'in the midst of' typically implies being surrounded by a group or crowd. For a structural bar positioned halfway up the height of the frames, 'in the middle of' or 'halfway up' is more idiomatic and precise for architectural descriptions.

revisions.from: that remains over
revisions.to: that remains
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'remains over' is slightly awkward and redundant in modern English. Using 'that remains' (which also maintains consistency with the earlier rendering of the same root) or 'that is left over' is more natural.

revisions.from: at the end in the first set joined together; and so you shall do on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second set joined together.
revisions.to: at the end of the first joined set; and so you shall do on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second joined set.
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'set joined together' is unnecessarily wordy and slightly clunky; 'joined set' is much more natural in English while accurately conveying the meaning of the Hebrew terms. Additionally, 'at the end in the first set' is awkward and reads more smoothly as 'at the end of the first joined set.'

revisions.from: separate for you between the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place
revisions.to: separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place for you
revisions.rationale: The phrasing 'separate between A and B' is a Hebraism (reflecting בֵּין... וּבֵין). In standard English, a divider 'separates A from B' or 'makes a separation between A and B'.

revisions.from: and there, inside the veil, you shall bring the ark of the testimony
revisions.to: and you shall bring the ark of the testimony there, inside the veil
revisions.rationale: Placing the lengthy locative phrase before the verb and direct object feels slightly clunky in English. Moving the direct object ('the ark of the testimony') closer to the verb ('bring') improves the flow.

revisions.from: or he is found in his hand
revisions.to: or if the man is found in his hand
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'or he is found in his hand' suffers from pronoun ambiguity in English, as 'he' refers to the victim and 'his' refers to the kidnapper, while the preceding verbs ('kidnaps', 'sells') have the kidnapper as the subject. Specifying 'the man' (or 'the victim') clarifies the shift in subject.

revisions.from: my hand will dispossess them
revisions.to: my hand will destroy them
revisions.rationale: While the Hiphil of ירשׁ often means 'dispossess' in the context of taking land, lexicons (such as HALOT) specifically categorize its use in Exodus 15:9 as 'destroy' or 'annihilate'. 'Dispossess' is contextually awkward here because the fleeing Israelites did not yet possess a land or territory to be dispossessed of; the Egyptian army's intent was to slaughter or re-enslave them.

revisions.from: made for them a statute and a judgment
revisions.to: established for them a statute and a rule
revisions.rationale: In English, 'made a judgment' strongly implies forming an opinion or deciding a specific court case, rather than instituting a standing legal norm. Using 'established' or 'set' with 'rule' or 'ordinance' better conveys the institution of a binding law.

revisions.from: out from
revisions.to: out of
revisions.rationale: While 'out from' is understandable, 'brought... out of' is the standard and more natural English idiom for this construction, and it perfectly captures the preposition מִן (min) attached to 'land'.

revisions.from: shall do it
revisions.to: shall keep it
revisions.rationale: While עָשָׂה (asah) literally means 'do' or 'make,' in English one does not 'do' a festival or ritual. 'Keep it' or 'observe it' is the standard and natural English idiom for עָשָׂה when referring to the Passover, accurately conveying the Hebrew meaning without sounding colloquial or vague.

revisions.from: the dwelling of the sons of Israel, during which they lived in Egypt
revisions.to: the time that the sons of Israel lived in Egypt
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'the dwelling... during which they lived' is redundant and awkward in English. The Hebrew construction (מוֹשַׁב ... אֲשֶׁר יָשְׁבוּ) uses a noun and verb of the same root. Rendering מוֹשַׁב as 'the time that' (or 'the period of residence') avoids the redundancy and produces a much more natural English sentence while accurately conveying the temporal meaning.

revisions.from: out from the land
revisions.to: out of the land
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'brought [them] out from' is slightly clunky and redundant in English. 'Brought [them] out of' is the standard, natural idiom and fully captures the source preposition (מִן).

revisions.from: this same night is a vigil for the LORD for all the children of Israel
revisions.to: this same night is for the LORD, a vigil for all the children of Israel
revisions.rationale: Separating the prepositional phrases better reflects the Hebrew word order (where 'for the LORD' precedes 'a vigil') and avoids the slightly clunky stacking of 'for the LORD for all the children of Israel'.

revisions.from: keep this matter
revisions.to: keep this command
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'keep a matter' is an unnatural collocation in English. While 'keep' is a standard translation for שׁמר and 'matter' is a valid gloss for דָּבָר, combining them here creates awkwardness. In English, one 'keeps a command/word/statute' or 'observes a rite/event.' Adjusting to 'keep this command' or 'observe this rite' resolves the clash while preserving the meaning.

revisions.from: each man according to his eating you shall count for the lamb.
revisions.to: you shall make your count for the lamb according to what each man can eat.
revisions.rationale: "According to his eating" is a wooden literalism that sounds unnatural in English. Furthermore, "you shall count for the lamb" is awkward and slightly ambiguous. Rephrasing to "make your count for the lamb" or "calculate for the lamb" clarifies that the counting/calculating is to determine how many people are needed per lamb based on their appetite.

revisions.from: pressed the people urgently to send them out of the land quickly
revisions.to: pressed the people, hurrying to send them out of the land,
revisions.rationale: The English syntax 'pressed [object] to [verb]' incorrectly makes 'the people' the subject of 'send' (implying the Israelites were sending someone out). The Egyptians are the implied subject of the infinitives. Additionally, the draft redundantly translates the single Hebrew word for haste (לְמַהֵר) as both 'urgently' and 'quickly'.

revisions.from: from days to days
revisions.to: from year to year
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew idiom מִיָּמִים יָמִימָה means 'from year to year' or 'annually'. Translating it literally as 'from days to days' results in awkward English that obscures the actual meaning, making it sound like a daily or sporadic occurrence rather than an annual festival. In optimal equivalence, the meaning of the idiom should be in the main text, with the literal Hebrew in the footnote.

revisions.from: “Lest the people change their minds when they see war and return to Egypt.”
revisions.to: “The people might change their minds when they see war and return to Egypt.”
revisions.rationale: Starting a direct quotation with the conjunction 'Lest' creates a grammatical fragment in English. It is smoother to either render the quote as an independent clause (e.g., 'The people might...') or to remove the quotation marks and treat it as indirect discourse ('for God said, lest the people...').

revisions.from: among man and among beast
revisions.to: both man and beast
revisions.rationale: In English, the preposition 'among' typically requires a plural or mass noun object. Using it with the singular collectives 'man' and 'beast' sounds unnatural ('among man'). The Hebrew construction (בְּ... וּבְ...) is standardly and idiomatically rendered as 'both... and...' or 'whether... or...' in English to express this inclusive categorization.

revisions.from: shook the Egyptians off into
revisions.to: shook the Egyptians into
revisions.rationale: While 'shook off' captures the literal Hebrew metaphor (נער, like shaking dust from a garment), combining it with the directional phrase 'into the midst of the sea' creates slightly awkward English syntax (one usually shakes something off *of* a surface). 'Shook the Egyptians into' or 'swept the Egyptians into' flows more naturally while retaining the violent, sudden action.

revisions.from: the great hand that the LORD had used
revisions.to: the great hand that the LORD had displayed / the great power that the LORD had used
revisions.rationale: The collocation 'used a hand' is slightly awkward in English. If retaining the literal 'hand' to preserve the Exodus motif, verbs like 'displayed,' 'shown,' or 'wielded' pair more naturally with it. Alternatively, translating 'hand' as 'power' or 'work' pairs better with 'used' or 'did' (as seen in many major translations).

revisions.from: Speak to the sons of Israel, so that they turn back
revisions.to: Tell the sons of Israel to turn back
revisions.rationale: While 'Speak... so that' is grammatically defensible for a waw + jussive following an imperative, it reads more like a purpose clause than an indirect command in English. 'Tell the sons of Israel to turn back' (or 'Speak to the sons of Israel, that they turn back') is much more natural for conveying a directive.

revisions.from: out from his land
revisions.to: out of his land
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'out of' is the standard, idiomatic English expression for movement exiting a place. It fully captures the preposition מִן (min) without the slight clunkiness of 'out from'.

revisions.from: on which they are
revisions.to: on which they stand
revisions.rationale: While 'on which they are' is a literal rendering of the verbless Hebrew clause, 'on which they stand' is more idiomatic in English and commonly used to translate this phrase, whether 'they' refers to the houses or the Egyptians.

revisions.from: and so that the fear of him may be before you, so that you may not sin.
revisions.to: and in order that the fear of him may be before you, so that you may not sin.
revisions.rationale: The repetition of 'so that' for two different Hebrew purpose constructions (וּבַעֲבוּר and לְבִלְתִּי) is slightly clunky in English. Varying the translation (e.g., 'in order that... so that...') improves readability while maintaining the precise purposive force of both clauses.

revisions.from: from the land of Egypt, from the house of slaves
revisions.to: out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slaves
revisions.rationale: The verb phrase 'brought out' typically pairs more naturally in English idiom with 'out of' rather than 'from' when referring to exiting a bounded area or condition (a land or a house).

revisions.from: with your wife and her two sons with her.
revisions.to: and your wife, and her two sons with her.
revisions.rationale: Translating the waw on 'your wife' as 'with' creates a slightly awkward repetition in English alongside the final 'with her' (עִמָּהּ). Rendering the initial conjunction as 'and' avoids the double 'with' while preserving the exact Hebrew phrasing and the sense of accompaniment.

revisions.from: after he had sent her away.
revisions.to: after Moses had sent her away. (or "after she had been sent away.")
revisions.rationale: The pronoun 'he' is grammatically ambiguous in English and naturally points back to the subject of the main clause (Jethro). To avoid implying that Jethro sent her away, it is better to either explicitly name Moses (if you prefer the active voice) or use the passive 'after she had been sent away' (which perfectly mirrors the Hebrew's implicit agent).

revisions.from: of whom the name of the one was Gershom
revisions.to: one of whom was named Gershom
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'of whom the name of the one' is overly wooden and awkward in English. Rendering the Hebrew relative clause as 'one of whom was named' or starting a new independent clause ('The name of one was...') flows much more naturally while preserving the exact meaning.

revisions.from: Then Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, came to Moses in the wilderness with his sons and his wife, where he was camped, at the mountain of God.
revisions.to: Then Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, came with his sons and his wife to Moses in the wilderness, where he was camped at the mountain of God.
revisions.rationale: Placing 'with his sons and his wife' after 'in the wilderness' separates the relative clause ('where he was camped') from its antecedent ('the wilderness'). Restoring the Hebrew word order ('came with his sons and his wife to Moses in the wilderness') resolves this awkward modifier placement.

revisions.from: there will be none on it.
revisions.to: there will be none.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew 'bo' (in/on it) functions as a resumptive pronoun for the fronted phrase 'on the seventh day'. In English, retaining 'on it' at the end of the clause is redundant and slightly awkward since 'on the seventh day' already establishes the temporal context.

revisions.from: and put there an omerful of manna
revisions.to: and put an omerful of manna in it
revisions.rationale: While שָׁמָּה literally means 'there' or 'thither', translating it as 'put there' results in awkward, slightly archaic English syntax. Rendering it as 'in it' or 'into it' is much more natural while accurately conveying the locative meaning of placing the manna inside the jar.

revisions.from: to fight against Amalek
revisions.to: and fought against Amalek
revisions.rationale: While literally an infinitive construct (לְהִלָּחֵם, 'to fight'), rendering it as an infinitive in English after 'did as Moses had told him' is awkward. Translating it as a coordinate verb ('and fought') is standard and much more natural in English.

revisions.from: and put it in Joshua’s hearing
revisions.to: and recite it to Joshua
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'put it in [someone's] hearing' is an unnatural collocation in English, resulting from a mix of the literal Hebrew ('put in the ears of') and a dynamic English idiom ('in the hearing of'). 'Recite it to Joshua' or 'read it to Joshua' conveys the meaning naturally, while the literal idiom is already well-handled in the footnote.

revisions.from: Why is this—that you brought us up
revisions.to: Why did you bring us up / Why is it that you brought us up
revisions.rationale: The construction 'Why is this—that' is highly unnatural in English and reads like translationese. While the Hebrew 'לָמָּה זֶּה' is emphatic, 'Why did you bring us up' or 'Why is it that you brought us up' captures the emphasis without breaking English syntax.

revisions.from: all the night
revisions.to: all night
revisions.rationale: While 'all the night' is a literal rendering of the Hebrew article (הַלָּיְלָה), 'all night' (or 'all that night') is much more natural in contemporary English and maintains the optimal-equivalence balance without losing meaning.

revisions.from: the sound of a trumpet, very strong
revisions.to: a very loud trumpet sound
revisions.rationale: In English, sounds are described as 'loud' rather than 'strong.' The post-positive phrasing 'the sound of a trumpet, very strong' is clunky. Translating חָזָק as 'loud' in the context of sound is standard, and the literal 'strong' is already well-handled by the existing footnote.

revisions.from: stronger and stronger
revisions.to: louder and louder
revisions.rationale: While 'stronger' reflects the literal Hebrew 'חָזֵק', English idiom strongly prefers 'louder' when describing a sound (קוֹל). Using 'stronger' for a sound feels slightly unnatural and creates a mixed collocation. Consider swapping the text and footnote, putting 'louder and louder' in the main text and noting the literal 'strong' in the footnote.

revisions.from: he shall let him go free in compensation for his tooth
revisions.to: he shall let the slave go free in compensation for the tooth
revisions.rationale: Because the condition explicitly names both a 'male slave' and a 'female slave,' reverting to the masculine singular pronouns ('him', 'his') in English creates slight friction. Rendering the suffix as 'the slave' resolves the gender mismatch and clarifies the referents (distinguishing the master 'he' from the slave).

revisions.from: according to the judges
revisions.to: as the judges determine
revisions.rationale: In English, 'according to the judges' typically means 'in the opinion of the judges' (e.g., 'According to the judges, he won'). To convey the intended legal assessment (בִּפְלִלִים), 'as the judges determine' or 'by the judges' decision' is much clearer and avoids the colloquial ambiguity.

revisions.from: do not give your heart to
revisions.to: do not take to heart
revisions.rationale: In English, 'giving your heart to' something strongly implies romantic affection or deep emotional devotion. The Hebrew idiom (נתן לב) means to pay attention, apply one's mind, or take seriously. 'Do not take to heart' or 'do not pay attention to' accurately conveys the meaning while avoiding the misleading English idiom, and 'take to heart' still retains the 'heart' imagery.

revisions.from: And I saw, I myself,
revisions.to: And I myself saw
revisions.rationale: Placing the emphatic pronoun after the verb as an appositive ('I saw, I myself') is awkward in English. 'And I myself saw' preserves the emphasis of the independent Hebrew pronoun (אָנִי) while using natural English syntax.

revisions.from: I have not regarded him,
revisions.to: I have not regarded them,
revisions.rationale: Although the Hebrew suffix on the verb is singular (likely agreeing with the nearest noun, 'father', or acting distributively), English requires a plural pronoun ('them') when referring back to the compound object 'his father and his mother.' Retaining 'him' makes the English sound grammatically incorrect and inadvertently excludes the mother from the statement.

revisions.from: with your waist belted
revisions.to: with your belt fastened
revisions.rationale: "Waist belted" is slightly unnatural in English. "With your belt fastened" or "belted at the waist" conveys the readiness of the Hebrew idiom more idiomatically while maintaining the concrete imagery.

revisions.from: shall be for you as a memorial
revisions.to: shall be a memorial for you
revisions.rationale: The preposition לְ (le) before 'memorial' indicates the predicate state ('be a memorial') and does not strictly require 'as a' in English. 'Shall be a memorial for you' is more natural and idiomatic while remaining entirely faithful to the Hebrew syntax.

revisions.from: and they did so, just as
revisions.to: and did just as
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'and they did so, just as' is slightly redundant and clunky in English. Rendering 'וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כֵן כַּאֲשֶׁר' simply as 'and did just as' or 'and did exactly as' improves the narrative flow while fully capturing the Hebrew emphasis on exact obedience.

revisions.from: Look, he is going out to the water, and you shall stand to meet him on the bank of the Nile, and you shall take
revisions.to: Look, he is going out to the water. Stand to meet him on the bank of the Nile, and take
revisions.rationale: The draft's punctuation obscures the fact that 'stand' and 'take' are commands continuing from 'Go'. Breaking the sentence after 'water' and using imperative verbs ('Stand... and take...') makes the English much more natural and restores the clear injunctive force of the Hebrew sequence.

revisions.from: Then the LORD said to Moses and to Aaron, saying,
revisions.to: Then the LORD said to Moses and to Aaron,
revisions.rationale: Translating both וַיֹּאמֶר as 'said' and לֵאמֹר as 'saying' creates a redundant Hebraism ('said... saying') that is awkward in modern English. In optimal-equivalence translations, לֵאמֹר is typically left untranslated (represented by a comma or colon) when the main verb is already rendered as 'said'.

revisions.from: Pharaoh sent,
revisions.to: Pharaoh sent word, / Pharaoh sent messengers,
revisions.rationale: While 'Pharaoh sent' is a literal rendering of the Hebrew, the verb 'send' is strongly transitive in English and usually expects an object. Supplying 'word' or 'messengers' (or 'men') makes the English much more natural while accurately conveying the implied object of the Hebrew verb.

revisions.from: was no longer poured down
revisions.to: no longer poured down
revisions.rationale: While the Niphal of נתך can be passive ('be poured out'), in the context of rain it is often translated intransitively in English ('pour down'). 'No longer poured down' is more natural and less clunky than the passive 'was no longer poured down.'

revisions.from: He said, “Tomorrow.” And he said,
revisions.to: He said, “Tomorrow.” Moses said,
revisions.rationale: Using 'he said' twice for two different speakers in rapid succession obscures the change of speaker in English. Supplying 'Moses' (or using 'he replied') clarifies the dialogue exchange.

revisions.from: over the land of Egypt
revisions.to: on the land of Egypt
revisions.rationale: While translating 'עַל' (al) consistently as 'over' matches the preceding phrases, the English collocation 'bring up... over' is slightly awkward and can sound as though the frogs are hovering. 'Bring up... on' or 'upon' is much more natural for causing something to emerge onto the land.

revisions.from: go a journey of three days
revisions.to: go a three days' journey
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'go a journey of three days' is slightly clunky in modern English. 'Go a three days' journey' or 'Make a three-day journey' flows more naturally while accurately reflecting the Hebrew idiom.

revisions.from: to cut off the frogs
revisions.to: to destroy the frogs
revisions.rationale: While 'cut off' is the literal root meaning of כרת, in English, 'cutting off' frogs implies physical mutilation rather than elimination or removal. 'Destroy' or 'remove' conveys the Hebrew meaning naturally without the awkward imagery.

revisions.from: he made households for them
revisions.to: he established households for them
revisions.rationale: In English, one does not typically 'make' a household. While 'make' is a literal rendering of עשה, when paired with 'households' (rather than physical 'houses'), verbs like 'established' or 'provided' represent normal English collocation and better convey the meaning.

revisions.from: Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.
revisions.to: Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.
revisions.rationale: The commas before 'and' break up the natural pairs indicated by the Hebrew text and make the English punctuation awkward. Removing the commas before the conjunctions better reflects the pairing of the names.

revisions.from: out from
revisions.to: out of
revisions.rationale: While 'out from' is understandable, 'out of' is the standard English idiom for exiting a region or container. It perfectly captures the ablative force of the preposition מִן (min) without the slight clunkiness of 'out from'.

revisions.from: this is Moses and Aaron
revisions.to: it is this Moses and Aaron
revisions.rationale: While 'this is' literally reflects the singular pronoun הוּא, 'it is this Moses and Aaron' (or 'it was this Moses and Aaron') better captures the emphatic, resumptive nature of the Hebrew and avoids the slight grammatical awkwardness of 'this is' with a plural compound subject.

revisions.from: the clans of the Levite
revisions.to: the clans of the Levites
revisions.rationale: While 'ha-levi' is a singular gentilic noun in Hebrew, translating it as 'the Levite' in English sounds like a reference to a single, specific unnamed individual rather than the collective tribe. 'The Levites' (or 'Levi') is the natural English rendering for this collective usage and avoids confusing the reader.

revisions.from: spoke this
revisions.to: said this
revisions.rationale: While the drafter's note correctly identifies that כֵּן points back to the preceding message, the collocation 'spoke this' is awkward in English. One typically 'says this,' 'speaks these words,' or 'speaks thus.'

revisions.from: such as has never happened before, nor will ever happen again.
revisions.to: such as has never been before, nor will ever be again.
revisions.rationale: The verb 'happen' does not collocate naturally with 'cry' (a cry does not 'happen'). Translating the Hebrew root היה as 'been'/'be' is both more literal and provides a smoother English reading.

revisions.from: ask from his neighbor, and each woman from her neighbor, for articles
revisions.to: ask his neighbor, and each woman her neighbor, for articles
revisions.rationale: In English, one typically 'asks [someone] for [something]' or 'requests [something] from [someone].' The combination 'ask from [someone] for [something]' is slightly awkward. Removing 'from' (or changing 'ask' to 'request' and dropping 'for') improves the natural flow while maintaining the meaning.

revisions.from: Not any green thing remained
revisions.to: No green thing remained
revisions.rationale: Translating the Hebrew negative particle plus 'kol' (all/every) as 'Not any' is grammatically accurate but results in clunky English. 'No green thing' is the standard, natural English equivalent that perfectly preserves the absolute negation.

revisions.from: one will not be able to see
revisions.to: no one will be able to see
revisions.rationale: While 'one will not be able to see' is grammatically correct, 'no one will be able to see' is a much more natural English idiom for expressing a general inability to see something.

revisions.from: ask from her neighbor and from the woman staying in her house articles of silver
revisions.to: ask her neighbor and the woman staying in her house for articles of silver
revisions.rationale: In English, the standard idiom is 'to ask [someone] for [something]' rather than 'to ask from [someone] [something].' Adjusting the prepositions makes the sentence flow much more naturally while accurately reflecting the Hebrew syntax.

revisions.from: Who has put a mouth on man?
revisions.to: Who has made man's mouth? / Who gives man a mouth?
revisions.rationale: While 'put a mouth on man' is a literal rendering of the Hebrew idiom (שָׂם פֶּה לָאָדָם), it sounds unnatural in English and evokes the physical attachment of a body part. Translating שׂים contextually as 'made' or 'gives' (e.g., 'Who has made man's mouth?') is much more idiomatic while still adhering to an optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: it had returned like his flesh
revisions.to: it was restored like his flesh
revisions.rationale: While 'returned' is the literal gloss for שָׁבָה, in English 'returned like his flesh' is awkward because it sounds as though the action of returning was similar to his flesh. When שׁוּב refers to healing or returning to a previous state (as in 2 Kings 5:14), 'restored' is the standard and most natural English rendering, accurately conveying the Hebrew idiom without sounding disjointed.

revisions.from: Is there not Aaron your brother, the Levite?
revisions.to: Is not Aaron the Levite your brother? / What about your brother Aaron, the Levite?
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing is slightly wooden and follows the Hebrew word order very closely. Adjusting it could yield a more natural English rhetorical question while preserving the exact meaning.

revisions.from: And also, look,
revisions.to: Besides, look, / And look,
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'And also, look' is slightly clunky. 'Besides, look' or simply 'And look' captures the additive and deictic force of 'וְגַם הִנֵּה' (ve-gam hinneh) more smoothly in modern English.

revisions.from: from before it
revisions.to: from it
revisions.rationale: "Fled from before it" is somewhat awkward translationese in modern English. "Fled from it" is much more natural. Furthermore, the current footnote ("Hebrew literally, 'from before it'") assumes that the main text is using a dynamic rendering, creating a mismatch since the text currently uses the literal rendering.

revisions.from: his officers
revisions.to: their officers
revisions.rationale: The 3rd person masculine singular suffix on שֹׁטְרָיו refers to the collective noun עָם (the people), not to Pharaoh. In modern English, 'his officers' strongly implies they are Pharaoh's officers, which contradicts the narrative (the shoterim are Israelites, cf. 5:14). Using 'their officers' (or 'its officers') correctly links them to the people.

revisions.from: So Pharaoh commanded, on that day, the taskmasters
revisions.to: That same day, Pharaoh commanded the taskmasters
revisions.rationale: Placing the temporal phrase between the verb and its direct object interrupts the natural flow of English. Moving the temporal phrase to the beginning of the sentence improves readability while maintaining the emphasis of the Hebrew word order.

revisions.from: You yourselves, go and get straw for yourselves
revisions.to: You, go and get straw for yourselves
revisions.rationale: Translating both the emphatic pronoun (אַתֶּם) and the reflexive preposition (לָכֶם) with 'yourselves' creates awkward repetition in English. Rendering the initial pronoun simply as 'You' maintains the emphatic force without the clunkiness.

revisions.from: nothing of your work will be reduced
revisions.to: none of your work will be reduced / your work will not be reduced at all
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'nothing of your work' is slightly unnatural in English. Using 'none of your work' or translating דָּבָר adverbially ('at all') produces a more idiomatic rendering.

revisions.from: They met Moses and Aaron, who were standing to meet them when they came out from Pharaoh.
revisions.to: As they came out from Pharaoh, they encountered Moses and Aaron, who were waiting to meet them.
revisions.rationale: The current draft repeats 'met/meet' in close proximity, which is stylistically clunky. Furthermore, placing the temporal clause ('when they came out') at the end creates slight pronoun ambiguity about whether Moses and Aaron or the foremen were coming out from Pharaoh. Moving the temporal clause to the beginning and varying the vocabulary resolves both issues.

revisions.from: stink in the eyes
revisions.to: stink in the sight
revisions.rationale: While accurately reflecting the Hebrew mixed metaphor, 'stink in the eyes' is particularly jarring in English. Rendering as 'stink in the sight' or 'made us a stench in the eyes' slightly softens the awkwardness while preserving the underlying idiom of perceived standing.

revisions.from: were beaten, saying,
revisions.to: were beaten and asked,
revisions.rationale: In English, the participle 'saying' grammatically modifies the subject of the main clause ('the Israelite foremen'), which incorrectly implies the foremen were speaking the quote. The Hebrew infinitive construct לֵאמֹר (saying) often introduces direct speech without strictly agreeing with the subject of the preceding passive verb. Rendering it as 'and asked' or 'with the taskmasters demanding' clarifies that the taskmasters are the ones speaking.

revisions.from: making them stop from
revisions.to: making them cease from / stopping them from
revisions.rationale: In English, 'stop from' followed by a noun ('burdens') is slightly awkward. 'Cease from' or 'stopping them from' is more idiomatic while still accurately reflecting the causative force of the Hiphil verb (הִשְׁבַּתֶּם).

revisions.from: in those many days
revisions.to: during those many days
revisions.rationale: 'During those many days' or 'in the course of those many days' is more natural in English while still accurately reflecting the Hebrew preposition בְּ (be).

revisions.from: crushed into the pit
revisions.to: crushed at the pit (or 'crushed at the cistern')
revisions.rationale: In English, 'crushed into' typically introduces the resulting state of the object (e.g., 'crushed into pieces' or 'crushed into dust'). Using it for directionality ('crushed [and falling] into the pit') reads awkwardly. Taking the preposition אֶל (el) as locative ('at' or 'by'), parallel to עַל (al) in the preceding clause, yields much more natural English. Additionally, while 'pit' is defensible for its death resonance, 'cistern' provides a much clearer parallel to 'spring' in the previous line.

revisions.from: and like firmly planted nails are the masters of collections
revisions.to: and the masters of collections are like firmly planted nails
revisions.rationale: The inverted syntax is awkward in English and breaks the natural parallelism with the first clause ('The words of the wise are like goads'). Standardizing the word order makes the proverb much clearer and easier to read.

revisions.from: the day of his birth
revisions.to: the day of one's birth
revisions.rationale: Although project guidelines prefer retaining the masculine pronoun, 'his' lacks an antecedent in this sentence, creating a dangling pronoun in English. 'One's birth' resolves the grammatical awkwardness while maintaining the singular, representative nature of the proverb.

revisions.from: For your heart also knows that many times you yourself have also cursed others.
revisions.to: For your own heart knows that many times you yourself have also cursed others.
revisions.rationale: The double use of 'also' makes the English sentence feel clunky and somewhat redundant. The first Hebrew גַּם (gam) serves to emphasize the subject's internal awareness (often rendered dynamically as 'your own heart knows' or by modifying 'many times' as 'even many times'), while the second emphasizes the reciprocal action. Adjusting the first 'also' improves the English flow while preserving the emphatic force of the source text.

revisions.from: they no longer have a share forever
revisions.to: they will never again have a share
revisions.rationale: The combination of 'no longer' and 'forever' is slightly clunky in English syntax. Rendering אֵין לָהֶם עוֹד לְעוֹלָם as 'they will never again have' is more idiomatic while fully preserving the temporal finality of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: laid all this to my heart
revisions.to: took all this to heart
revisions.rationale: While 'laid to my heart' is a literal reflection of the Hebrew idiom, 'took to heart' is the standard English equivalent and flows much more naturally without losing the optimal-equivalence standard.

revisions.from: during their lives—and after it,
revisions.to: during their lives—and afterward,
revisions.rationale: Because 'lives' is plural in English, 'after it' creates an awkward pronoun disagreement. Rendering אַחֲרָיו as 'afterward' or 'after that' is standard, resolves the English grammatical clash, and maintains the abruptness of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: that he has given you
revisions.to: that God has given you
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew verb leaves the subject implicit (3ms), English requires an explicit subject. Using the pronoun 'he' without an antecedent in the immediate context creates awkward English. Supplying 'God' (or using a passive like 'that have been given to you') resolves the grammatical issue while accurately reflecting the understood Hebrew subject.

revisions.from: where they had done so
revisions.to: where they had done such things
revisions.rationale: In English, 'done so' acts as a pro-verb phrase that naturally points back to the most recent actions in the sentence (coming, going, or being forgotten). Since the intended meaning refers to their general wicked behavior in the city, 'done such things' or 'acted thus' avoids this confusing grammatical linkage.

revisions.from: a vanity that is done
revisions.to: a vanity that takes place
revisions.rationale: In English, 'vanity' (referring here to a futile or incongruous state of affairs) is not typically something that is 'done.' Rendering the Niphal of עשה as 'takes place' or 'occurs' is more natural in this context and avoids an awkward collocation while maintaining the optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: its master
revisions.to: its possessor
revisions.rationale: While 'master' is a standard literal gloss for בַּעַל, 'its master' applied to 'wickedness' in English awkwardly implies someone who has mastered a skill or acts as an overlord. The Hebrew idiom refers to the one who possesses or is given over to it. 'Its possessor' or 'those who practice it' conveys the meaning much more naturally in English.

revisions.from: Going toward the south and turning toward the north, turning, turning goes the wind
revisions.to: The wind goes toward the south and turns toward the north; turning, turning goes the wind
revisions.rationale: Delaying the subject ('the wind') until the second clause creates a long string of fronted participles that makes the English slightly awkward and difficult to parse. Introducing the subject in the first clause improves readability while maintaining the optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: and even of the later ones who will be, there will be no remembrance of them
revisions.to: and even of the later ones who will be, there will be no remembrance
revisions.rationale: The draft translates the Hebrew left-dislocation and resumptive pronoun ('to the later ones... to them') literally, resulting in a redundant and awkward English construction ('of the later ones... of them'). Removing 'of them' or restructuring the clause improves readability while maintaining optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: Even on the road, when the fool walks, his sense is lacking,
revisions.to: Even when the fool walks on the road, he lacks sense,
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing strictly follows the Hebrew word order but results in a slightly disjointed English clause. Placing 'on the road' after 'walks' and rendering the idiom as 'he lacks sense' (or 'his sense fails him') creates a much smoother, more natural English sentence while maintaining optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: their heart
revisions.to: their hearts
revisions.rationale: While Hebrew frequently uses a singular noun with a plural suffix for body parts (בְּלִבָּם), English typically requires the plural 'hearts' when referring to multiple individuals, unless referring to a singular collective entity. 'Their hearts' reads more naturally in English.

revisions.from: they may fear before him
revisions.to: people may fear before him
revisions.rationale: The pronoun 'they' lacks a clear antecedent in the English text. Rendering the unexpressed subject of the third-person plural verb as 'people' clarifies the general human subject implied by the Hebrew.

revisions.from: that God is testing them, and to show them
revisions.to: that God is testing them and showing them
revisions.rationale: Coordinating a finite 'that' clause ('that God is testing them') with an infinitive phrase ('and to show them') creates awkward English syntax. Changing to 'and showing them' (or 'and that they might see') restores grammatical parallelism while preserving the intended meaning.

revisions.from: What has been already is
revisions.to: What has been, already is
revisions.rationale: Without a comma, 'already' naturally attaches to 'has been' in the reader's mind, creating a garden-path sentence that leaves 'is' dangling. Adding a comma clarifies the syntax and reflects the Hebrew cantillation (the disjunctive Pashta on הָיָה).

revisions.from: all the living, those walking
revisions.to: all the living who walk
revisions.rationale: While 'those walking' accurately reflects the substantival participle, rendering it as a relative clause ('who walk') smooths out the English syntax without losing any meaning or formal equivalence.

revisions.from: the robbery of justice and righteousness
revisions.to: the denial of justice and righteousness
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew word 'gezel' literally means robbery or violent seizure, 'robbery of justice' is an awkward collocation in English. 'Denial,' 'violation,' or 'perversion' of justice are much more natural English idioms that convey the violent stripping away of rights intended by the Hebrew.

revisions.from: and he fathered a son and had nothing in his hand.
revisions.to: he fathered a son, but had nothing in his hand.
revisions.rationale: The repeated use of 'and' creates a slightly run-on sentence. Replacing the second 'and' with 'but' (or rendering the final clause as 'and there is nothing in his hand') improves the English flow and highlights the tragic contrast of having an heir but no inheritance to leave him.

revisions.from: nor whoever loves abundance with income
revisions.to: nor will whoever loves abundance be satisfied with income
revisions.rationale: The elision of 'will be satisfied' creates a garden-path sentence where it sounds like the person 'loves abundance with income.' Repeating the verb (or adding a comma: 'nor whoever loves abundance, with income') clarifies the parallel structure.

revisions.from: in which I labored
revisions.to: for which I labored
revisions.rationale: In English, one typically labors 'for' or 'at' a task or outcome rather than 'in' it. 'For which I labored' slightly improves the naturalness of the preposition while maintaining the cognate repetition.

revisions.from: place these words of mine on your heart and on your soul
revisions.to: put these words of mine in your heart and in your soul
revisions.rationale: While 'place... on' is a literal rendering of the preposition עַל, 'placing' words 'on a soul' is highly unnatural in English. The idiom שׂים עַל לֵב generally means to take to heart or lay up in the mind/heart. Using 'put... in' or 'lay up... in' provides a much more natural English collocation while maintaining the optimal-equivalence balance.

revisions.from: All their labor that they imposed on them was with harshness.
revisions.to: They imposed all this labor on them with harshness.
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'was with harshness' is awkward and unidiomatic in English. Restructuring the sentence to make the imposition of labor the active verb (or adding 'done') improves the flow while accurately reflecting the Hebrew summary clause.

revisions.from: one of whom was named Shiphrah and the second Puah
revisions.to: one of whom was named Shiphrah and the other Puah
revisions.rationale: In English, the natural correlative to 'one' when referring to a pair is 'the other' rather than 'the second.' While 'the second' is a literal rendering of the Hebrew 'ha-shenit', mixing 'one of whom' with 'the second' creates a slightly disjointed English clause.

revisions.from: And beyond that, because Qoheleth was wise, he still taught
revisions.to: Besides being wise, Qoheleth also taught
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase וְיֹתֵר שֶׁ (wəyōṯêr še) functions together as a compound conjunction meaning 'besides that' or 'in addition to.' Splitting it into 'And beyond that, because...' misconstrues the grammar and alters the meaning. The text is saying 'Not only was he wise, he also taught,' rather than making his wisdom merely the cause of his teaching. (Consequently, עוֹד is better rendered 'also' here rather than 'still').

revisions.from: and also bread is not to the wise
revisions.to: nor is bread to the wise
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'and also bread is not' is clunky and disrupts the poetic rhythm established by the preceding clauses. Rendering 've-gam lo' as 'nor is' or 'nor yet' maintains the flow while accurately translating the Hebrew.

revisions.from: All is as it is for all:
revisions.to: It is the same for everyone:
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'All is as it is for all' is overly literal and results in clunky English. 'It is the same for everyone' or 'Everything is the same for all' better captures the Hebrew idiom (הַכֹּל כַּאֲשֶׁר לַכֹּל) in natural English while maintaining optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: it was great to me
revisions.to: it seemed great to me
revisions.rationale: "Great to me" is a highly literal rendering of גְדוֹלָה הִיא אֵלָי, but it is slightly awkward in English for expressing a personal impression or evaluation. "Seemed great to me" or "was significant to me" is more natural and accurately conveys the evaluative force of the preposition.

revisions.from: is authority
revisions.to: is authoritative
revisions.rationale: While 'authority' correctly translates the noun שִׁלְטוֹן, saying a word 'is authority' is slightly awkward in English. Rendering it as the adjective 'is authoritative' (or the phrase 'has authority') produces much more natural English while preserving the exact meaning.

revisions.from: to its place it pants
revisions.to: it hastens to its place
revisions.rationale: In English, 'pant' is a verb of respiration, not directed motion, making 'pants to its place' unidiomatic. While 'pant' captures the vivid metaphor of שׁוֹאֵף, it cannot naturally take a directional 'to' phrase. If the breathless imagery must be retained, 'panting for its place' or 'panting, it hurries to its place' would work better. Otherwise, 'hastens to its place' is the most natural rendering.

revisions.from: the sight of the eyes
revisions.to: what the eyes see
revisions.rationale: In English, 'the sight of the eyes' can easily be misunderstood as referring to the faculty of vision (i.e., not being blind). The Hebrew idiom here contrasts enjoying what is presently visible and attained with the restless craving for what is not. Promoting the footnote 'what the eyes see' to the main text clarifies this meaning and avoids the ambiguity while remaining faithful to the source.

revisions.from: at his right hand, but a fool's heart at his left
revisions.to: to his right, but a fool's heart to his left
revisions.rationale: Rendering 'at his right hand' can awkwardly imply to modern readers that the physical organ is located in the person's hand. 'To his right' (as noted in the footnote) maintains the Hebrew spatial idiom, avoids the anatomical confusion, and perfectly balances 'to his left' in the second clause.

revisions.from: Then I turned and saw another vanity
revisions.to: Again I saw vanity
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew construction of שׁוּב (shuv) followed by another verb (here וָאֶרְאֶה) is a standard idiom meaning 'to do [the second verb] again.' The lexical note incorrectly claims 'again' is not explicit; the verb שׁוּב itself functions as the adverb here. Translating it literally as 'turned' and inserting 'another' to compensate for the repetition misses the grammatical function of the idiom and creates an awkward count-noun usage of 'vanity'.

revisions.from: though even in his kingdom he had been born poor
revisions.to: even though he had been born poor in his kingdom
revisions.rationale: The construction 'though even' is slightly clunky in English. Rendering כִּי גַּם as 'even though' and adjusting the word order yields much more natural English syntax while perfectly preserving the concessive force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: someone overpowers one, two will stand against him
revisions.to: one overpowers him, two will stand against him
revisions.rationale: The draft treats 'the one' (הָאֶחָד) as the object and supplies 'someone' as the subject. However, grammatically and poetically, 'the one' is the subject (the attacker) contrasting with 'the two' (הַשְּׁנַיִם) in the next clause. Translating 'if one overpowers him, two will stand against him' restores the Hebrew's syntactic symmetry and resolves the awkward pronoun pileup in the English draft.

revisions.from: to irrigate from them a grove sprouting trees
revisions.to: from which to irrigate a grove of growing trees
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'to irrigate from them a grove' retains a clunky Hebrew word order; 'from which to irrigate' is much more natural in English. Additionally, 'growing trees' is preferred over 'sprouting trees,' as 'sprouting' often implies only the initial stage of a plant's life, whereas the Hebrew 'צמח' here refers to the ongoing flourishing of the trees in the grove.

revisions.from: great herds of cattle and flocks
revisions.to: great herds and flocks
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'herds of cattle and flocks' is slightly awkward in English, as 'flocks' either becomes the object of 'herds of' (a category error) or is left unbalanced. Translating 'מִקְנֶה בָקָר וָצֹאן' simply as 'herds and flocks' or 'abundant livestock, both herds and flocks' is more natural.

revisions.from: labored in it
revisions.to: labored for it
revisions.rationale: While 'in it' is a literal rendering of בּוֹ, 'labored in it' is awkward in English when referring to the fruits of one's labor. 'Labored for it' is the natural English idiom and accurately conveys the Hebrew meaning.

revisions.from: his heart does not lie down
revisions.to: his heart does not rest
revisions.rationale: Translating שָׁכַב literally as 'lie down' when applied to the heart creates an unnatural English phrase. The Hebrew idiom refers to the heart (mind/emotions) finding rest or sleep. 'Rest' is the standard and clear English equivalent that fits the optimal-equivalence philosophy without sounding wooden. The literal rendering can be kept in the footnote.

revisions.from: by the way of which
revisions.to: by the route about which
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'by the way of which' is clunky and slightly ambiguous in English. Using 'route' or 'journey' with 'about which' (or simply 'by the way that') clarifies that the Lord is referring to the path or manner of their return.

revisions.from: and his judgments with Israel.
revisions.to: and his judgments, along with Israel.
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing ambiguously attaches 'with Israel' to 'his judgments' (which could imply judgments *against* or *concerning* Israel). Adding a comma and/or 'along/together' clarifies that Gad carried out these judgments in concert with the rest of Israel, matching the translator's stated rationale.

revisions.from: over him to afflict him with their burdens, and he built
revisions.to: over them to afflict them with their burdens, and they built
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses singular pronouns to agree with the singular collective noun 'people' (עַם) from verse 9. In English, 'people' takes plural pronouns. Translating literally as 'him' and 'he' violates English collective noun agreement and confuses the reader by making it sound like an individual man is being enslaved and building cities.

revisions.from: your graves
revisions.to: your grave
revisions.rationale: Although the Hebrew noun is plural in form (likely referring to a family tomb complex or acting as a plural of local extension), English requires the singular 'grave' when referring to a single individual's burial. 'Your graves' implies Josiah will be buried in multiple locations.

revisions.from: or if the latter man dies, who took her to himself as a wife—
revisions.to: or if the latter man, who took her to himself as a wife, dies—
revisions.rationale: In English, placing the relative clause ('who took her...') after the verb ('dies') creates an awkward separation from its antecedent ('the latter man'). Moving 'dies' to the end of the clause improves the flow and clarity without losing any source meaning.

revisions.from: sleep with his pledge
revisions.to: sleep in his pledge
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'sleep with' is a strong idiom for sexual intimacy. While the inanimate object ('pledge') prevents genuine misunderstanding, the phrasing remains slightly awkward or distracting. Translating the preposition ב as 'in' ('sleep in his pledge') is both more literal to the Hebrew and historically accurate, as the pledge was typically a cloak used as a blanket (as clarified in the following verse).

revisions.from: make his wife whom he has taken rejoice
revisions.to: bring joy to his wife whom he has taken
revisions.rationale: Placing the relative clause 'whom he has taken' between the causative verb 'make' and its complement 'rejoice' creates a slightly clunky English construction. Rendering the Piel of שׂמח as 'bring joy to' or 'make glad' allows the object and its modifier to sit naturally at the end of the sentence without splitting the verbal idea.

revisions.from: from the daughters of Israel, and there shall not be a cult prostitute from the sons of Israel
revisions.to: among the daughters of Israel, and there shall not be a cult prostitute among the sons of Israel
revisions.rationale: While 'from' is a literal rendering of the preposition מִן (min), 'among' or 'of' is more natural in English existential prohibitions of this type, avoiding the slightly wooden phrasing of 'from the daughters/sons'.

revisions.from: your eye shall not pity
revisions.to: your eye shall show no pity
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'pity' is almost exclusively a transitive verb. Using it intransitively ('shall not pity') sounds archaic or incomplete. 'Show no pity' or 'have no pity' retains the literal Hebrew idiom ('your eye') while using natural English grammar.

revisions.from: from on his foot
revisions.to: from his foot
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'from on' is an overly literal rendering of the Hebrew compound preposition מֵעַל (me'al). In English, 'from his foot' or 'off his foot' is much more natural and conveys the exact same spatial relationship without the clunkiness.

revisions.from: your days are drawing near for you to die
revisions.to: the time for you to die is drawing near
revisions.rationale: While literally reflecting the Hebrew idiom (קָרְבוּ יָמֶיךָ לָמוּת), 'your days are drawing near for you to die' is slightly clunky in English. Rendering it as 'the time for you to die is drawing near' or 'the days are drawing near for you to die' improves the flow while preserving the optimal-equivalence balance.

revisions.from: The LORD your God himself is crossing over before you. He himself will destroy these nations from before you, and you will dispossess them. Joshua himself is crossing over before you
revisions.to: The LORD your God himself is crossing over before you. He will destroy these nations from before you, and you will dispossess them. Joshua is crossing over before you
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses the independent pronoun הוּא (he) in all three clauses for focus or as a copula, rendering it as 'himself' three times in English is overly heavy and unnatural. Reducing the repetition of 'himself' improves the English flow while still preserving the emphatic focus on the LORD and Joshua.

revisions.from: destroy these nations from before you
revisions.to: destroy these nations before you
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'destroy... from before you' is a literal rendering of a Hebraism (verb of destruction + מִלְּפָנֶיךָ). In English, one typically 'removes from before' or 'destroys before.' Changing to 'before you' is more natural while retaining the spatial sense of the nations being cleared out of Israel's path.

revisions.from: uncovered his father's covering
revisions.to: uncovered his father's garment
revisions.rationale: Translating כָּנָף as 'covering' creates an awkward repetition in English ('uncovered... covering') that does not exist in the Hebrew roots (גִלָּה and כָּנָף). Using 'garment' or 'skirt' avoids this repetition while accurately reflecting the literal meaning of כָּנָף.

revisions.from: all the commandment
revisions.to: the whole commandment
revisions.rationale: In English, using 'all' directly before a singular count noun (like 'commandment') is awkward. 'The whole commandment' or 'the entire commandment' is much more natural while still accurately reflecting the singular collective Hebrew phrasing (כָּל הַמִּצְוָה).

revisions.from: become engaged to a wife
revisions.to: become engaged to a woman
revisions.rationale: In English, one becomes engaged to a 'woman' (who then becomes a wife). Saying 'engaged to a wife' sounds like she is already married. While the Hebrew 'ishah' can mean either 'woman' or 'wife', 'woman' is the natural choice here when paired with the modern phrasing 'become engaged to'.

revisions.from: in the lack of everything
revisions.to: for lack of everything
revisions.rationale: "In the lack of everything" is awkward in English. "For lack of everything" is the standard, idiomatic way to express this causal/circumstantial phrase (בְּחֹסֶר כֹּל) while maintaining optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: The tender and delicate woman among you, who has not ventured to set the sole of her foot on the ground because of delicacy and tenderness, her eye will be evil toward
revisions.to: The tender and delicate woman among you, who has not ventured to set the sole of her foot on the ground because of delicacy and tenderness, will look with an evil eye toward
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the Hebrew left-dislocation ('The woman... her eye...') creates a dangling subject in English. Adjusting the predicate to 'will look with an evil eye toward' (or 'will be hostile toward') resolves the grammatical clunkiness while preserving the idiom and meaning.

revisions.from: the rain of your land
revisions.to: rain for your land
revisions.rationale: While 'the rain of your land' strictly follows the Hebrew construct state, 'rain for your land' is more natural in English and accurately conveys the genitive relationship (destination/benefit) without losing formal equivalence.

revisions.from: you will be only oppressed
revisions.to: you will be nothing but oppressed
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'you will be only oppressed' is slightly clunky. 'You will be nothing but oppressed' captures the restrictive force of 'אַךְ' (akh) in a more natural English idiom.

revisions.from: treated with contempt the Rock of his salvation
revisions.to: scorned the Rock of his salvation
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'treated with contempt' is slightly clunky when placed before the longer object 'the Rock of his salvation.' A single-word verb like 'scorned' or 'despised' maintains the poetic rhythm and active dishonoring sense more naturally, or alternatively, reordering to 'treated the Rock of his salvation with contempt' would improve the English flow.

revisions.from: There is none like God, O Jeshurun, who rides through the heavens to help you, and in his majesty through the skies.
revisions.to: O Jeshurun, there is none like God, who rides through the heavens to help you, and in his majesty through the skies.
revisions.rationale: Placing the vocative 'O Jeshurun' immediately before the relative clause 'who rides...' creates a misplaced modifier in English, making it sound momentarily as if Jeshurun is the one riding the heavens. Moving the vocative to the beginning of the sentence resolves this syntactic ambiguity while preserving the meaning.

revisions.from: may it come
revisions.to: may they come
revisions.rationale: Because the English translation renders מֶגֶד as the plural 'choicest things' and pairs it with 'favor,' the singular pronoun 'it' creates an awkward grammatical disagreement in English. Using 'may they come' or 'let these come' smooths the English syntax while preserving the collective jussive force of the Hebrew verb.

revisions.from: tears off arm, even scalp
revisions.to: tears off an arm, even the scalp
revisions.rationale: In English, singular count nouns like 'arm' and 'scalp' typically require articles. While omitting them can sound poetic or raw, adding 'an' and 'the' (or 'the' for both) makes the phrasing more natural without losing the literal force of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: gore peoples together to the ends of the earth
revisions.to: gore the peoples, all of them, to the ends of the earth
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'gore peoples together' is awkward and could be misread as goring them into a single mass. Adding the definite article ('the peoples') and rendering יַחְדָּו (yachdav) as 'all of them' or 'all together' clarifies the collective scope of the action without sounding unnatural.

revisions.from: you shall not go over the branches after yourself
revisions.to: you shall not go over the branches again
revisions.rationale: Translating אַחֲרֶיךָ literally as 'after yourself' results in an awkward English idiom. Rendering it as 'again,' 'afterward,' or 'a second time' conveys the agricultural reality (not making a second pass) much more naturally while remaining faithful to the text.

revisions.from: sin in you
revisions.to: sin for you
revisions.rationale: While literal to the preposition בְ, 'sin in you' is unnatural in English for expressing culpability. 'Sin for you' or 'guilt for you' better captures the legal and moral standing.

revisions.from: turn back
revisions.to: turn around
revisions.rationale: In English, 'turn back' strongly implies returning to one's starting point (e.g., heading back to camp), which confuses the sequence of events. 'Turn around' (or simply 'turn') accurately conveys the physical action of turning to face the hole to cover the excrement.

revisions.from: and you shall go out there, outside.
revisions.to: and you shall go out there.
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering 'there, outside' (שָׁמָּה חוּץ) is redundant and clunky in English. The directional and locational sense is fully captured by 'go out there' or 'go outside to it' without the awkward repetition.

revisions.from: entering there to possess it.
revisions.to: entering to possess it.
revisions.rationale: In English, 'entering there' is redundant and awkward. The directional sense of שָׁמָּה is already captured by the verb 'entering,' making 'there' unnecessary for optimal equivalence.

revisions.from: appoint commanders of the armies at the head of the people
revisions.to: appoint commanders of the armies to lead the people
revisions.rationale: In English, 'appoint [someone] at the head of' is awkward and can sound like the physical location where the appointing takes place. Rendering the idiom בְּרֹאשׁ as 'to lead' or 'over' clarifies the role. (Additionally, you may want to consider an impersonal passive—'commanders of the armies shall be appointed'—since many commentators note it is unlikely the civil officers were the ones appointing the military commanders).

revisions.from: his eye will be evil toward
revisions.to: he will be hostile toward
revisions.rationale: While preserving the Hebrew idiom is a valid optimal-equivalence choice, 'his eye will be evil toward' is archaic and somewhat clunky in modern English. If the idiom must be preserved, 'he will look with an evil eye toward' flows better; otherwise, 'he will be hostile toward' or 'he will begrudge' communicates the meaning more naturally.

revisions.from: sharpen the lightning of my sword
revisions.to: sharpen my flashing sword
revisions.rationale: While 'lightning' is the primary meaning of בָּרָק, in the context of a weapon it refers to its flash or glittering blade. In English, 'sharpening lightning' creates a mixed metaphor that obscures the action. 'Sharpen my flashing sword' or 'sharpen the flashing blade of my sword' is much more natural while preserving the intended imagery.

revisions.from: return vengeance
revisions.to: render vengeance
revisions.rationale: 'Return vengeance' is a very literal rendering of the Hiphil of שׁוּב with נָקָם, but it is not a standard English idiom. 'Render vengeance' or 'bring vengeance' better captures the Hebrew idiom without sounding like a calque.

revisions.from: and with the yield of the months
revisions.to: and with the choicest yield of the months
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew word מֶגֶד (choicest/precious things) is repeated in the second half of the verse (וּמִמֶּגֶד) but was omitted in the English translation.

revisions.from: to your afflicted one, and to your poor one
revisions.to: to your afflicted and to your poor
revisions.rationale: Adding 'one' to these substantival adjectives makes the English unnecessarily clunky. 'Your afflicted' and 'your poor' function perfectly well as substantives in English, maintaining the personal connection of the pronominal suffix without the awkwardness of 'one.'

revisions.from: make a release
revisions.to: grant a release
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'make a release' is somewhat archaic and awkward in contemporary English (sounding almost like a software deployment). 'Grant a release' is the standard English collocation for this legal action and accurately reflects the functional force of the Hebrew verb עָשָׂה (to do/perform/enact) in this context.

revisions.from: work with the firstborn of your ox
revisions.to: do work with the firstborn of your ox
revisions.rationale: In modern English, the verb phrase 'to work with [an animal]' often implies training or cooperating with the animal rather than using it for agricultural labor. Rendering it as 'do work with' (treating 'work' as a noun) or simply 'work the firstborn of your ox' (transitive verb) clarifies the intended meaning of the Hebrew instrumental/associative בְּ.

revisions.from: send him out free from you
revisions.to: let him go free from you
revisions.rationale: While 'send him out' reflects the literal force of שׁלח, 'let him go free' is the standard English idiom for manumission and flows more naturally with the prepositional phrase 'from you.'

revisions.from: every abomination of the LORD
revisions.to: every abomination to the LORD
revisions.rationale: While 'abomination of the LORD' formally reflects the Hebrew construct state, in English it can awkwardly imply that the LORD possesses or commits the abomination. 'Abomination to the LORD' accurately conveys the relational/objective sense of the construct and is standard English idiom, avoiding potential misunderstanding.

revisions.from: that the LORD your God chooses to put his name there
revisions.to: where the LORD your God chooses to put his name
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew relative particle אֲשֶׁר (asher) paired with the adverb שָׁם (sham) functions as 'where' in English. Translating both as 'that... there' creates an awkward, ungrammatical resumptive pronoun structure in English.

revisions.from: that you are living
revisions.to: that you live
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses a participle (חַיִּים), the English progressive 'are living' is slightly clunky in this standard temporal idiom. 'That you live' is more natural and still fully captures the ongoing state of life on the earth.

revisions.from: and you shall not spare,
revisions.to: and you shall not spare him,
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew verb lacks an explicit object here, 'spare' in English is typically transitive in this context. Supplying 'him' improves the natural flow and matches the surrounding clauses.

revisions.from: the place that the LORD your God will choose to put his name there
revisions.to: the place where the LORD your God will choose to put his name
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a resumptive adverb (שָׁם, "there") which is standard grammar for Hebrew relative clauses ("the place which... there"). In English, retaining "there" after "the place that" is redundant and clunky. Using "where" (or simply dropping "there") produces much more natural English while preserving the exact meaning and optimal-equivalence standard.

revisions.from: And the Levite shall come, because he has no portion or inheritance with you, and the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow who are within your gates, and they shall eat and be satisfied
revisions.to: And the Levite, because he has no portion or inheritance with you, and the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow who are within your gates, shall come and eat and be satisfied
revisions.rationale: The literal word order separates the compound subjects ('the Levite... and the sojourner...') from the verbs they share, making the English syntax disjointed and slightly ambiguous. Grouping the subjects before the verbs ('shall come and eat and be satisfied') clarifies the sentence structure while preserving all elements of the source text.

revisions.from: You may eat this from all that are
revisions.to: You may eat these from all that are / You may eat this from all that is
revisions.rationale: Using the singular 'this' alongside the plural 'all that are' creates a slight grammatical discord in English. If retaining the singular 'this' to reflect the Hebrew 'zeh', 'all that is' sounds more natural (and matches the singular 'everything that has' in the next clause). Alternatively, 'these' is the standard English idiom when referring to a plural category of items.

revisions.from: this case is like when a man rises against his neighbor and murders him.
revisions.to: this case is like that of a man who rises against his neighbor and murders him. (Or: "For just as a man rises against his neighbor and murders him, so is this case.")
revisions.rationale: The phrasing "like when" is somewhat colloquial and feels slightly awkward in the formal register of biblical case law. Adjusting to "like that of a man who" or restoring the Hebrew "just as... so" (כַּאֲשֶׁר... כֵּן) structure elevates the English while remaining highly accurate to the source.

revisions.from: that is lost from him and you find it
revisions.to: that is lost from him and that you find
revisions.rationale: Retaining the Hebrew resumptive pronoun ('it') at the end of the relative clause creates awkward English syntax. Dropping 'it' and repeating the relative pronoun ('and that you find') produces natural English while accurately reflecting the Hebrew structure.

revisions.from: the land that he swore to our fathers
revisions.to: the land that he swore to give to our fathers
revisions.rationale: In English, one typically swears an oath or swears to do something; 'swearing a land' is slightly elliptical. Adding 'to give' (as many major translations do) smooths out the English while maintaining the covenantal weight of 'swore', though the current literal rendering is also defensible.

revisions.from: But from the cities
revisions.to: But in the cities
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew preposition מִן (min) here functions partitively or to specify the domain of the command. Rendering it strictly as 'from' creates an awkward English construction with the verb 'let live' (implying extraction). 'In the cities' or 'Of the cities' is more natural in English while accurately reflecting the Hebrew idiom.

revisions.from: and it makes war
revisions.to: but makes war
revisions.rationale: Repeating the pronoun 'it' makes the conditional clause slightly clunky. Translating the vav on וְעָשְׂתָה as an adversative 'but' (or simply dropping the second 'it' to read 'and makes war') yields more natural English that highlights the contrast.

revisions.from: the matter does not happen or come about, that is the matter that the LORD has not spoken
revisions.to: the word does not happen or come about, that is the word that the LORD has not spoken
revisions.rationale: Translating דָּבָר (dabar) as 'matter' creates an awkward English collocation with the verb 'spoken' ('the matter that the LORD has not spoken'). Using 'word' or 'message' restores natural English phrasing and preserves the Hebrew cognate relationship between the noun דָּבָר (word) and the verb דִּבֶּר (speak).

revisions.from: from him
revisions.to: of him
revisions.rationale: While 'from him' accurately reflects the Hebrew preposition (מֵעִמּוֹ), the standard English idiom is typically to 'require [something] of [someone].'

revisions.from: to do according to the abominations
revisions.to: to imitate the abominations
revisions.rationale: While 'do according to' is a faithful, literal rendering of the Hebrew infinitive with the comparative preposition (לַעֲשׂוֹת כְּ), it is slightly wooden in English. Rendering it as 'imitate' or 'follow' provides a more natural English collocation while preserving the optimal-equivalence goal of expressing conformity to those practices.

revisions.from: crossing over there to possess
revisions.to: crossing over to possess
revisions.rationale: The directional 'there' (שָׁמָּה) is already implied in the English phrasal verb 'crossing over' and the following infinitive 'to possess.' Retaining 'there' makes the English sound slightly colloquial or redundant. Omitting it (or rendering as 'crossing into') improves the natural flow without losing the source's meaning.

revisions.from: that the LORD will choose to make his name dwell there
revisions.to: where the LORD will choose to make his name dwell
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a standard relative clause with a resumptive pronoun (אֲשֶׁר ... שָׁם, lit. 'which ... there') to express 'where'. In English, combining 'that' and 'there' in this continuous clause is syntactically awkward. Rendering it as 'where... dwell' (or adding a comma: 'that the LORD will choose, to make his name dwell there') resolves the clunkiness while accurately reflecting the Hebrew grammar.

revisions.from: never return again
revisions.to: never return / not return again
revisions.rationale: "Never return again" is slightly redundant in English. Using either "never return" or "not return again" conveys the emphatic prohibition more naturally.

revisions.from: by this way
revisions.to: this way / by this route
revisions.rationale: "By this way" sounds slightly archaic. "This way" or "by this route" is more natural in modern English while accurately reflecting the Hebrew.

revisions.from: to keep all the words of this law and these statutes, to do them.
revisions.to: to keep all the words of this law and these statutes, and do them.
revisions.rationale: In English, appending 'to do them' as an asyndetic infinitive phrase feels slightly clunky. Adding 'and' (or rendering as 'by doing them') smooths the syntax while preserving the epexegetical force of the Hebrew infinitive construct (לַעֲשֹׂתָם).

revisions.from: to testify against him of wrongdoing
revisions.to: to accuse him of wrongdoing
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'testify against him of [something]' is slightly awkward and archaic in modern English. While 'testify' is a good gloss for ענה, 'accuse him of wrongdoing' (or 'testify against him concerning wrongdoing') provides a much more natural English collocation for this legal context.

revisions.from: purge the innocent blood
revisions.to: purge the guilt of innocent blood
revisions.rationale: In English, 'purging the innocent blood' can sound as though the innocent blood itself is the undesirable thing being removed. Adding 'the guilt of' (as already clarified in your footnote) prevents this misunderstanding while maintaining the legal force of the idiom.

revisions.from: and though they discipline him, he does not listen to them,
revisions.to: and who, though they discipline him, does not listen to them,
revisions.rationale: The draft introduces a relative clause ('who does not listen...') but then switches to an independent clause ('he does not listen...') for the second condition. Adding 'who' maintains parallel structure and makes the English syntax flow more smoothly within the 'If' protasis.

revisions.from: his place
revisions.to: his town
revisions.rationale: In modern English, "his place" strongly connotes a person's private residence (e.g., "let's go back to his place"). Translating מָקוֹם (maqom) as "town" or "hometown" here avoids this colloquial distraction while accurately conveying the local civic meaning. The literal "place" can be relegated to the footnote.

revisions.from: remove the clothing of her captivity from herself
revisions.to: remove the clothing of her captivity
revisions.rationale: In English, the verb 'remove' applied to one's own clothing already implies taking it off oneself. Adding 'from herself' is redundant and slightly clunky. Alternatively, 'take off the clothing of her captivity' naturally captures the force of the Hebrew preposition (מֵעָלֶיהָ) without sounding wooden.

revisions.from: because you shall do
revisions.to: because you must do
revisions.rationale: Combining the causal 'because' with the prescriptive 'shall' creates an awkward English clause ('because you shall do'). If the intent is prescriptive (as noted in the lexical decision), 'because you must do' is much more natural. Alternatively, translating כִּי as 'when' ('when you do') is a common and natural way to render this clause.

revisions.from: who of all flesh
revisions.to: who is there of all flesh / who among all flesh
revisions.rationale: While 'who of all flesh' is grammatically permissible, adding 'is there' (as in 'who is there of all flesh') or 'among' smooths out the English phrasing and matches the typical rendering of this Hebrew construction.

revisions.from: Take there one of the priests
revisions.to: Take one of the priests there
revisions.rationale: Placing the locative adverb 'there' between the verb and its direct object is awkward in English. Moving 'there' after the object (or using 'Send one of the priests there') creates a more natural English clause while preserving the meaning.

revisions.from: uncover his father’s covering
revisions.to: uncover his father’s garment
revisions.rationale: "Uncover his father's covering" is redundant and clunky in English. Additionally, כָּנָף (kanaf) specifically refers to the "wing," "edge," or "skirt" of a garment rather than a general "covering." Using "garment" or "skirt" avoids the awkward repetition while accurately preserving the Hebrew metaphor.

revisions.from: as the gazelle and the deer
revisions.to: as you would a gazelle or a deer
