Rephrasing to 'that has seed-bearing fruit in it' or 'with fruit that bears seed' would improve flow and readability without sacrificing fidelity to the source.

revisions.from: the chief of the prison
revisions.to: the prison warden
revisions.rationale: While 'chief of the prison' is a literal rendering of the Hebrew components (שַׂר בֵּית הַסֹּהַר), it is slightly unnatural in contemporary English. A more idiomatic title for this role would be 'the prison warden' or 'the chief jailer'.

revisions.from: And it happened, as soon as Isaac had finished blessing Jacob, that just as Jacob had barely gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, Esau his brother came in from his hunting.
revisions.to: And it happened that as soon as Isaac had finished blessing Jacob and Jacob had barely gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, Esau his brother came in from his hunting.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering uses a pile-up of temporal conjunctions ('as soon as,' 'that just as') which makes the sentence structure awkward and difficult to parse. The Hebrew uses a paratactic structure (wayhî... wayhî... we...) to narrate a rapid sequence of events. The suggested rewrite smooths out the English syntax by combining the first two events into a single subordinate clause ('as soon as... and...'), which more clearly and naturally sets the stage for the main event of Esau's arrival.

revisions.from: so that I may eat, in order that
revisions.to: and I will eat, so that
revisions.rationale: The sequence of a cohortative verb (וְאֹכֵלָה) followed by a purpose clause (בַּעֲבוּר) is rendered here with two consecutive purpose clauses ('so that... in order that...'), which is slightly redundant and stylistically heavy in English. Translating the cohortative as a simple future ('and I will eat') creates a more natural flow that reflects the sequence of actions leading to the ultimate purpose: 'bring it to me and I will eat, so that my soul may bless you.'

revisions.from: Please do not let my lord be angry
revisions.to: Please, my lord, do not be angry
revisions.rationale: While grammatically possible, the phrase 'do not let my lord be angry' is a slightly stilted and indirect construction in English. A more natural and direct address, such as 'Please, my lord, do not be angry' or 'Please do not be angry, my lord,' would better reflect the direct plea in the Hebrew (אַל־נָא יִחַר לַאדֹנָי) and improve the flow of the dialogue.

revisions.from: five of the fifty righteous are lacking
revisions.to: the fifty righteous are lacking five
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing makes 'five' the subject, which could be misread as five specific people being absent from a group of fifty. The Hebrew syntax (`יַחְסְרוּן חֲמִשִּׁים הַצַּדִּיקִם חֲמִשָּׁה`) has 'the fifty righteous' as the subject, indicating that the required number (50) is deficient by an amount (5). The suggested rewrite restores this subject-verb relationship, clarifying that the total number of righteous people is 45.

revisions.from: There, in the tent.
revisions.to: She is in the tent.
revisions.rationale: The rendering of הִנֵּה בָאֹהֶל as 'There, in the tent' is slightly stilted, particularly with the comma. The particle הִנֵּה presents the answer to the 'where' question emphatically. A more natural English equivalent would be to supply the implied subject and verb, as in 'She is in the tent,' which is common in modern translations (e.g., ESV, NIV). This avoids the awkwardness while still conveying the locative information clearly.

revisions.from: lived, after he fathered Eber, 403 years
revisions.to: lived 403 years after he fathered Eber
revisions.rationale: The current word order, which mirrors the Hebrew syntax, is unnatural in English. It separates the verb 'lived' from its modifying phrase of duration '403 years' with an intervening clause. Reordering to 'lived 403 years after he fathered Eber' makes the sentence flow more naturally and clearly connects the action to its duration, which is standard English syntax.

revisions.from: Arpachshad lived, after he fathered Shelah, 403 years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
revisions.to: Arpachshad lived 403 years after he fathered Shelah, and he fathered sons and daughters.
revisions.rationale: The current word order, while closely following the Hebrew syntax, is awkward in English. It separates the verb 'lived' from its duration '403 years' with the intervening clause 'after he fathered Shelah'. This makes the sentence slightly difficult to parse. Reordering the clauses to place the duration immediately after the verb ('lived 403 years') creates a more natural and clear English sentence without changing the meaning.

revisions.from: let it be according to your words: the one with whom it is found will become my slave, and you will be innocent.
revisions.to: let it be according to your words. The one with whom it is found will become my slave, and you will be innocent.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering makes it sound as if the steward is accepting the brothers' proposal from verse 9 (death for the thief, slavery for the rest). The use of a colon implies that what follows is an explanation or restatement of 'your words.' However, the steward is actually rejecting their harsh terms and substituting his own more lenient (but still severe) ones. He is countering, not agreeing. This misrepresents the rhetorical force of his speech. Changing the colon to a period correctly presents the second clause as the steward's own decree, which the reader can then see is a modification of the brothers' offer. This is supported by the major disjunctive accent (athnah) on הוּא in the Hebrew, which indicates a primary pause in the verse.

revisions.from: when the flesh of his foreskin was circumcised
revisions.to: when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin
revisions.rationale: The current rendering makes 'the flesh of his foreskin' the grammatical subject of the passive verb 'was circumcised.' This is awkward and shifts the focus from Ishmael, who is the logical subject of the action in the Hebrew (indicated by the suffix on the infinitive בְּהִמֹּלוֹ, 'in his being circumcised'). The suggested rewrite maintains Ishmael as the one being acted upon and correctly renders אֵת בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתוֹ as an accusative of respect ('in the flesh of his foreskin'), which is more syntactically faithful, clearer English, and aligns with the rendering in most formal equivalence translations.

revisions.from: in the earth
revisions.to: on the earth
revisions.rationale: The rendering 'in the earth' is a literal translation of the Hebrew preposition בְּ, but it is slightly unnatural in English, where it can sometimes imply being underground. The more common idiom for this context is 'on the earth,' which is also a valid translation of the Hebrew and better conveys the sense of influence and activity upon the world's surface.

revisions.from: the coastlands of the nations were divided
revisions.to: the peoples of the coastlands spread out
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing makes it sound as if the land itself was partitioned, which is awkward. The Hebrew verb נִפְרְדוּ is more naturally applied to people separating or spreading out (cf. Gen 13:9, 11). Translating the subject אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם as 'the peoples of the coastlands' (a valid interpretation) and the verb as 'spread out' clarifies that the verse describes the dispersion of peoples, not just a geographical division.

revisions.from: between us, between us and you
revisions.to: between our two parties—between you and us
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew `בֵּינוֹתֵינוּ בֵּינֵינוּ וּבֵינֶךָ` uses a somewhat redundant construction for formal emphasis. While the current rendering is a literal representation, it reads as clunky in English. A phrasing like "between our two parties—between you and us" or even collapsing it to "between us and you" could capture the formal, bilateral nature of the Hebrew more idiomatically without sacrificing meaning.

revisions.from: Surely, look, she is your wife.
revisions.to: Why, she is your wife!
revisions.rationale: While `אַךְ` ('Surely') and `הִנֵּה` ('look') are rendered literally, stacking two such interjections is somewhat unnatural in English. A more integrated phrasing like 'Why, she is your wife!' or 'So she is your wife after all!' would capture Abimelech's surprised realization more smoothly.

revisions.from: Please, my lords, turn aside, please,
revisions.to: My lords, please turn aside,
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text contains two particles of entreaty (נָּא), which the draft renders with two instances of 'please'. While faithful to the repetition in the source, this results in a slightly awkward and unnatural-sounding phrase in English. Combining the force of the entreaty into a single 'please' improves the flow while still conveying the intended tone of polite urgency.

revisions.from: And it happened, when God destroyed the cities of the plain, that
revisions.to: So when God destroyed the cities of the plain,
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew construction `וַיְהִי בְּ... וַ...` is a common way to introduce a temporal setting. The draft's literal rendering 'And it happened... that...' is grammatically correct but creates a stilted and less direct English sentence. A more idiomatic rendering that captures the discourse function of `וַיְהִי` is to use 'So when...' or simply 'When...', making the sentence flow more naturally and bringing the main clause forward.

revisions.from: out from the midst of
revisions.to: out of the midst of
revisions.rationale: While 'out from' is not incorrect, 'out of' is the more common and idiomatic preposition in this context of extraction ('sent Lot out of the midst of...'). This change would slightly improve the naturalness of the English phrasing.

revisions.from: pressed hard against the man, against Lot, very much
revisions.to: pressed very hard against the man, against Lot
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew adverb מְאֹד (me'od) intensifies the verb וַיִּפְצְרוּ (vayyiftsru). The draft's rendering, "pressed hard... very much," is redundant and stylistically awkward in English. Combining the verb and its intensifier into the more natural English phrase "pressed very hard" conveys the same meaning with better syntax and flow.

revisions.from: And Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him, Isaac.
revisions.to: And Abraham called the name of his son Isaac—the one who was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering mirrors the Hebrew word order, which separates the act of naming ('called the name of his son') from the name itself ('Isaac') with two subordinate clauses. This creates a syntactically complex and slightly cumbersome sentence in English. Moving 'Isaac' to directly follow 'his son' would make the main clause clearer and improve the overall readability without altering the meaning or lexical choices.

revisions.from: about which I commanded you
revisions.to: which I commanded you about
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing 'about which I commanded you' is grammatically correct but feels formal and slightly stilted in modern English. Rephrasing to 'which I commanded you about' is more natural and improves the flow of the sentence without altering the meaning.

revisions.from: and asks you, saying,
revisions.to: and asks you,
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'asks you, saying,' is a literal translation of the Hebrew `וִשְׁאֵלְךָ לֵאמֹר`. While formally accurate, the word 'saying' is redundant in English when introducing a question after the verb 'to ask'. Removing it creates a more natural and fluid sentence without sacrificing meaning.

revisions.from: and all the household of Joseph, and his brothers, and his father's household. Only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds they left in the land of Goshen.
revisions.to: and all the household of Joseph, and his brothers, and his father's household; they left only their little ones, their flocks, and their herds in the land of Goshen.
revisions.rationale: The current draft is broken into two sentences. The first is a sentence fragment, as it's a list of noun phrases without a verb. The second sentence uses an awkward word order ('Only [objects] they left...') that directly mimics the Hebrew syntax but is unnatural in English. Combining these into a single sentence, linked by a semicolon, and rearranging the second clause into standard English word order ('they left only [objects]...') resolves both the grammatical fragment and the awkwardness, resulting in a more readable and fluid verse.

revisions.from: which Abraham had bought with the field
revisions.to: the field that Abraham had bought
revisions.rationale: The current rendering 'which Abraham had bought with the field' is grammatically awkward and misrepresents the Hebrew. It could be misinterpreted to mean 'the field' was the currency used for the purchase. The Hebrew syntax, with a relative pronoun ('which') followed by a clause that restates the object ('the field'), is a resumptive construction. The object of 'bought' (קָנָה) is explicitly 'the field' (אֶת הַשָּׂדֶה). The draft's attempt to resolve this creates confusion. A clearer and more accurate rendering uses an appositive phrase: '...the field of Machpelah, the field that Abraham had bought...' This correctly identifies what was purchased and resolves the Hebrew construction into natural English.

revisions.from: Chariots also went up with him, and horsemen
revisions.to: Chariots and horsemen also went up with him
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew גַּם...גַּם (gam...gam) construction links 'chariots' and 'horsemen' as a coordinate pair. The draft's rendering separates them with 'also... and', making 'horsemen' seem like an afterthought. The suggested rewrite joins them as a single subject, which is smoother in English and more faithful to the Hebrew pairing.

revisions.from: for look, the land
revisions.to: for, you see, the land
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'for look' is a literal but unnatural rendering of the Hebrew 'וְ... הִנֵּה'. While it attempts to capture the introductory and attention-grabbing force of 'הִנֵּה', it is jarring in contemporary English. A smoother alternative like 'for, you see,' or even just letting 'for' carry the logical connection ('for the land is broad...') would improve readability without losing the sense.

revisions.from: They took captive all their wealth, all their little ones, and their wives, and they plundered everything that was in the house.
revisions.to: They took captive all their little ones and their wives, and plundered all their wealth and everything that was in the house.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering illogically applies the verb 'took captive' (Heb. שָׁבָה), which is used for people, to 'wealth' (Heb. חֵיל). While the Hebrew syntax fronts a mixed list of objects before the verbs, a clear English rendering should associate the verbs with their appropriate objects. The suggested rewrite reorders the elements to correctly map the actions to the objects: 'took captive' applies to the people, while 'plundered' (Heb. בָּזַז) applies to all the goods ('wealth' and 'everything in the house'). This restructuring preserves the full meaning while creating a more natural and semantically accurate English sentence.

revisions.from: lived, after he fathered Noah, five hundred and ninety-five years
revisions.to: lived five hundred and ninety-five years after he fathered Noah
revisions.rationale: The current word order follows the Hebrew syntax closely but is awkward in English, as it separates the verb 'lived' from its duration ('five hundred and ninety-five years') with an intervening clause. Reordering the phrases to place the duration immediately after the verb creates a more natural and readable English sentence without changing the meaning.

revisions.from: who do his word by obeying the sound of his word
revisions.to: who do his word, listening to the sound of his word
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'do his word by obeying' is tautological, as 'doing his word' is a form of obedience. The Hebrew construction, with the participle 'doing' (עֹשֵׂי) followed by the infinitive 'to hear' (לִשְׁמֹעַ), suggests that hearing is the trigger or circumstance for the action, not a separate means. The suggested rewrite, which aligns with the provided footnote, removes the redundancy and more accurately reflects the Hebrew sequence of 'hear then do'.

revisions.from: among man
revisions.to: among humankind
revisions.rationale: While the drafter's rationale for using 'man' generically is noted, the specific phrasing 'among man' is awkward in contemporary English. A more natural and equally accurate rendering of the collective singular אָדָם would be 'among humankind' or 'among mortals.' This avoids both awkwardness and the potential for misinterpretation as gender-specific, which aligns better with the goals of an optimal-equivalence translation for a modern audience.

revisions.from: Your tongue devises destruction
revisions.to: You devise destruction; your tongue is
revisions.rationale: The current rendering incorrectly makes 'your tongue' the subject of 'devises'. In the Hebrew, the verb 'devises' (תַּחְשֹׁב) is second-person masculine singular, and its subject is the implied 'you' (the wicked person being addressed). 'Your tongue' (לְשׁוֹנֶךָ) is the subject of the following verbless clause, which is marked by the main poetic caesura (the Athnach accent): 'your tongue [is] like a sharpened razor'. The draft's syntax, while common in older translations, misrepresents the grammatical structure of the Hebrew, which distinguishes between the agent ('you') who plots and the instrument ('your tongue') that acts.

revisions.from: They will pour him out by the power of the sword; they will become a portion for foxes.
revisions.to: They will be poured out by the power of the sword; they will become a portion for foxes.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text presents a grammatical difficulty, shifting from a singular object ('him', from יַגִּירֻהוּ) in the first clause to a plural subject ('they', from יִהְיוּ) in the second. The current draft's literal rendering creates a misleading sentence in English, where the 'they' in the second clause seems to refer to the executioners from the first clause, not the victims. The intended meaning is that the enemies (plural, context from v. 10) are the ones who meet this fate. Using a passive construction ('They will be poured out') makes the subject consistent across both clauses, clarifying that the enemies are the ones being acted upon. This avoids misinterpretation and aligns with the common scholarly consensus on how to render the sense of this verse in English.

revisions.from: they pass away
revisions.to: it sprouts anew
revisions.rationale: The verb יַחֲלֹף (yaḥălōp) is used here, and its root (חלף) appears again in verse 6a paired with 'flourishes' (יָצִיץ), indicating a meaning of 'is renewed' or 'sprouts.' Rendering it as 'pass away' in v. 5 creates a contradiction, suggesting the grass both dies and flourishes in the morning. The correct poetic image is of grass that sprouts fresh in the morning (v. 5b-6a) only to wither by evening (v. 6b). The current rendering misses this crucial context. Additionally, the verb is singular, agreeing with 'grass,' so 'it' is more accurate than 'they'.

revisions.from: they are like sleep
revisions.to: they are like a dream
revisions.rationale: While a literal possibility for the terse Hebrew 'שֵׁנָה יִהְיוּ' (sleep they are), the phrase 'they are like sleep' is not idiomatic English for expressing transience. The intended metaphor, common in ancient literature and reflected in many modern translations (e.g., ESV, NRSV), is that of life's brevity being like a fleeting dream. 'Like a dream' is a clearer and more natural rendering of the underlying metaphor.

revisions.from: Do not give the life of your dove to the wild beast
revisions.to: Do not give your dove to the ravenous beast
revisions.rationale: The current translation, 'Do not give the life of your dove to the wild beast,' is a common rendering but requires interpreting the Hebrew construct form `לְחַיַּת` as if it were the absolute `לְחַיָּה` ('to the wild beast'), effectively setting aside the Masoretic pointing. The drafter's own lexical notes point to a more faithful interpretation of the Masoretic Text, where `חַיַּת נֶפֶשׁ` is a single unit meaning 'ravenous beast' (literally, 'beast of appetite'). Adopting this reading gives the more concise and powerful translation, 'Do not give your dove to the ravenous beast.' This reading is grammatically smoother, requires no emendation, and avoids the slight redundancy of 'the life of your dove.' The contradiction between the current translation and its own supporting rationale is a significant flaw.

revisions.from: like a dog
revisions.to: like dogs
revisions.rationale: While the Hebrew uses a representative singular (כַּכָּלֶב), the English rendering 'they growl like a dog' creates a slight grammatical awkwardness due to the plural subject 'they'. For optimal equivalence and naturalness in the target language, harmonizing to the plural 'like dogs' is preferable and reflects the sense of a pack of enemies more smoothly. The current rendering is defensible but less idiomatic.

revisions.from: your foot may crush in blood
revisions.to: your foot may crush them in blood
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verb 'timḥaṣ' (crush) is transitive. The current English rendering uses it intransitively, resulting in the awkward phrase 'crush in blood,' which is unidiomatic. Supplying the implied object 'them' (the enemies), as the existing footnote already suggests, resolves the awkwardness and clarifies the meaning without departing from the sense of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: the miscarriage of a woman who have not seen the sun
revisions.to: a stillborn child that has not seen the sun
revisions.rationale: The current rendering incorrectly attaches the relative clause 'who have not seen the sun' to 'a woman'. This is logically impossible and misrepresents the Hebrew. The phrase 'בַּל חָזוּ שָׁמֶשׁ' ('they have not seen the sun') refers to the 'נֵפֶל' (miscarriage/stillborn), not the mother. The plural verb 'חָזוּ' with the singular noun 'נֵפֶל' is a case of agreement by sense (constructio ad sensum), referring to the unborn. The draft's attempt to resolve this creates a significant error. A smoother, more accurate rendering would use a term like 'stillborn child' and apply the clause correctly, as many translations do and as the footnote itself suggests.

revisions.from: and unloaded the camels, and he gave
revisions.to: and Laban unloaded the camels and gave
revisions.rationale: The current rendering implies that 'the man' (Abraham's servant) performed the acts of hospitality for himself and his party. The Hebrew verbs וַיְפַתַּח (and he unloaded) and וַיִּתֵּן (and he gave) are 3ms and, while they could grammatically continue the subject 'the man', contextually they refer to the host, Laban (who in v. 31 says 'I have prepared the house'). The draft's rendering misrepresents the crucial host-guest dynamic of the narrative by making the guest the agent. Supplying 'Laban' as the subject, as many major translations do (e.g., ESV, NASB), is the clearest way to convey the correct meaning and avoid misleading the reader, which is a key goal of an 'optimal-equivalence' philosophy.

revisions.from: which Abraham bought with the field
revisions.to: the field that Abraham bought
revisions.rationale: The current rendering incorrectly translates the Hebrew direct object `אֶת הַשָּׂדֶה` ('the field') as a prepositional phrase ('with the field'). This changes the grammatical structure, leaving the verb 'bought' without its object and altering the meaning. The Hebrew text states that Abraham bought 'the field'. While the Hebrew syntax is redundant by English standards (using a relative pronoun and then repeating the object), the translation must preserve the core meaning. The suggested rewrite recasts the clause as an appositive ('...in the land of Canaan, the field that Abraham bought...'), which is a common and clear way to handle this Hebrew construction in English. This both corrects the grammatical error and resolves the potential ambiguity of the long chain of relative clauses.

revisions.from: These were the sons of Basemath
revisions.to: These were the grandsons of Basemath
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew word בְּנֵי (bene) can mean 'sons,' 'grandsons,' or 'descendants.' While the individuals listed are indeed the 'sons' of Reuel, the context established in Genesis 36:10 shows that Reuel is the son of Basemath. Therefore, the men listed in this verse are Basemath's grandsons. Translating the second instance of בְּנֵי as 'sons' creates a genealogical contradiction, making it appear that these men were simultaneously the sons of Reuel and the sons of Basemath. The rendering 'grandsons' is necessary for genealogical accuracy and clarity in English.

revisions.from: stolen my heart
revisions.to: deceived me
revisions.rationale: The English idiom 'to steal someone's heart' almost exclusively carries romantic connotations (to make someone fall in love with you). The Hebrew idiom 'גָּנַב לֵב' (ganav lev), however, means 'to deceive, to trick, to act stealthily behind someone's back.' By rendering the Hebrew idiom literally, the translation introduces a strong and incorrect romantic overtone into Laban's angry accusation. This is a significant mistranslation of the idiom's force and meaning. The footnote correctly identifies the alternative, but the main text is actively misleading to the average English reader. Optimal equivalence should prioritize conveying the correct meaning of the source idiom over a literal rendering that has a conflicting meaning in the target language.

revisions.from: I have given every green plant for food.
revisions.to: I give every green plant for food.
revisions.rationale: The verb 'I have given' is supplied from the context of verse 29 (נָתַתִּי). While 'I have given' (perfect tense) is a valid rendering of the Hebrew qatal, in this context of a divine decree, the English simple present 'I give' often carries a more immediate, performative, and timeless sense that fits the genre of creation commands well. This is a stylistic suggestion for improving the force of the statement.

revisions.from: because they had defiled their sister
revisions.to: because their sister had been defiled there
revisions.rationale: The current rendering creates a severe ambiguity where the pronoun 'they' most naturally refers to the subject of the main clause, 'The sons of Jacob.' This incorrectly implies that Jacob's sons were the ones who defiled their sister, which is the opposite of the narrative's meaning. The Hebrew clause `אֲשֶׁר טִמְּאוּ אֲחוֹתָם` functions as the reason for plundering the city, indicating that the defilement was perpetrated by the city's inhabitants. The suggested rewrite, 'because their sister had been defiled there,' resolves this critical ambiguity by using the passive voice and the adverb 'there,' correctly linking the defilement to the city without misidentifying the perpetrators.

revisions.from: So he took all these for himself
revisions.to: So he brought all these to him
revisions.rationale: Hebrew לוֹ in context refers to God — Abram is obeying the command 'Bring me...' from v9 (קָחָה לִי). Reflexive 'for himself' is grammatically possible but contextually unlikely; ESV, NIV, NRSV all read 'to him'.

revisions.from: and they will enslave them and afflict them
revisions.to: and they will be enslaved and afflicted
revisions.rationale: Active 'they will enslave them' creates pronoun ambiguity in English — the nearest antecedent ('sojourners') makes the reading backwards. Passive rewrite resolves the ambiguity while preserving the Hebrew's meaning (v.14 will clarify who does the enslaving).

revisions.from: began and planted a vineyard
revisions.to: began to plant a vineyard
revisions.rationale: Hebrew wa-yaḥel...wa-yiṭṭaʿ — two consecutive imperfects where the first verb (began) modifies the second (planted). Standard English rendering of this hendiadys is 'began to plant' (ESV, NIV, NRSV).

revisions.from: in which there is the breath of life
revisions.to: in which there is a living creature
revisions.rationale: Hebrew is נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה (nephesh chayyah), which means 'living creature/being' and is consistently rendered so in Gen 1:20, 1:21, 1:24. 'Breath of life' is the standard English for a DIFFERENT Hebrew phrase, נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים (nishmat chayyim), found in Gen 2:7. GPT-5.4's rendering conflated the two phrases.

revisions.from: and they went out with them
revisions.to: and they set out together
revisions.rationale: The MT wa-yeṣʾu ittam is a known textual crux — subject 'they' and object 'them' refer to overlapping people. 'They set out together' captures the sense without the confusing self-reference. (SP and LXX reflect a variant with single-subject phrasing.)

revisions.from: You will not surely die.
revisions.to: You will surely not die.
revisions.rationale: Hebrew לֹא מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן — negation of the infinitive-absolute + finite-verb construction. The emphatic force attaches to the DENIAL (the serpent's flat contradiction of Gen 2:17's מוֹת תָּמוּת). GPT-5.4's 'not surely die' is ambiguous in English ('maybe won't die'). Moving 'surely' inside the negation preserves the serpent's absolute-denial rhetoric.

revisions.from: walked with God after he fathered Methuselah three hundred years
revisions.to: walked with God for three hundred years after he fathered Methuselah
revisions.rationale: The current English syntax is ambiguous — a reader can parse 'fathered Methuselah three hundred years' as duration of fathering, which contradicts v21's statement that Enoch was 65 at Methuselah's birth. The Hebrew is unambiguous: three hundred years is the duration of Enoch's walk with God.

revisions.from: you may surely eat,
revisions.to: you may surely eat.
revisions.rationale: Quoted speech ending the verse requires a period inside the closing quote, not a comma (standard English punctuation for declarative quoted sentences).

revisions.from: This time, this one is bone from my bones
revisions.to: This at last is bone of my bones
revisions.rationale: זֹאת הַפַּעַם (zoʾt happaʿam) is an idiomatic exclamation of fulfillment/discovery — 'This at last!' or 'Now, finally!' (cf. ESV, NRSV, JPS). GPT-5.4's literal parsing 'This time, this one' is doubly redundant in English and misses the joyful-discovery force.
